title: Fall of Rome

infobox.value: or the **fall of Rome**, was the loss of central political control in the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), a process in which the Empire failed to enforce its rule, and its vast territory was divided among several successor [polities](/wiki/Polity "Polity"). The [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") lost the strengths that had allowed it to exercise effective control over its Western [provinces](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province"); modern historians posit factors including the effectiveness and numbers of the [army](/wiki/Late_Roman_army "Late Roman army"), the health and numbers of the Roman population, the strength of the [economy](/wiki/Roman_economy "Roman economy"), the competence of the [emperors](/wiki/Roman_emperor "Roman emperor"), the internal struggles for power, the religious changes of the period, and the efficiency of the civil administration. Increasing pressure from invading peoples outside Roman culture also contributed greatly to the collapse. [Climatic changes](/wiki/Climate_variability_and_change "Climate variability and change") and both [endemic](/wiki/Endemic_(epidemiology) "Endemic (epidemiology)") and [epidemic](/wiki/Epidemic "Epidemic") diseases drove many of these immediate factors.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017-1) The [reasons for the collapse](/wiki/Historiography_of_the_fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Historiography of the fall of the Western Roman Empire") are major subjects of the [historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography") of the ancient world and they inform much modern discourse on [state failure](/wiki/State_failure "State failure").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard-Perkins20051-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The **fall of the Western Roman Empire**, also called the **fall of the Roman Empire** or the **fall of Rome**, was the loss of central political control in the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), a process in which the Empire failed to enforce its rule, and its vast territory was divided among several successor [polities](/wiki/Polity "Polity"). The [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") lost the strengths that had allowed it to exercise effective control over its Western [provinces](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province"); modern historians posit factors including the effectiveness and numbers of the [army](/wiki/Late_Roman_army "Late Roman army"), the health and numbers of the Roman population, the strength of the [economy](/wiki/Roman_economy "Roman economy"), the competence of the [emperors](/wiki/Roman_emperor "Roman emperor"), the internal struggles for power, the religious changes of the period, and the efficiency of the civil administration. Increasing pressure from invading peoples outside Roman culture also contributed greatly to the collapse. [Climatic changes](/wiki/Climate_variability_and_change "Climate variability and change") and both [endemic](/wiki/Endemic_(epidemiology) "Endemic (epidemiology)") and [epidemic](/wiki/Epidemic "Epidemic") diseases drove many of these immediate factors.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017-1) The [reasons for the collapse](/wiki/Historiography_of_the_fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Historiography of the fall of the Western Roman Empire") are major subjects of the [historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography") of the ancient world and they inform much modern discourse on [state failure](/wiki/State_failure "State failure").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard-Perkins20051-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In 376, a large migration of [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths") and other non-[Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") people, fleeing from the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns"), [entered the Empire](/wiki/Gothic_War_(376%E2%80%93382) "Gothic War (376–382)"). Roman forces were unable to exterminate, expel or subjugate them (as was their normal practice). In 395, after winning two destructive civil wars, [Theodosius I](/wiki/Theodosius_I "Theodosius I") died. He left a collapsing field army, and the Empire divided between the warring ministers of his two incapable sons. Goths and other non-Romans became a force that could challenge either part of the Empire. Further barbarian groups crossed the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") and other frontiers. The armed forces of the Western Empire became few and ineffective, and despite brief recoveries under able leaders, central rule was never again effectively consolidated.

By 476, the position of Western Roman Emperor wielded negligible military, political, or financial power, and had no effective control over the scattered Western domains that could still be described as Roman. [Barbarian kingdoms](/wiki/Barbarian_kingdoms "Barbarian kingdoms") had established their own power in much of the area of the Western Empire. In 476, the [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") barbarian king [Odoacer](/wiki/Odoacer "Odoacer") deposed the last emperor of the Western Roman Empire in Italy, [Romulus Augustulus](/wiki/Romulus_Augustulus "Romulus Augustulus"), and the [Senate](/wiki/Roman_Senate "Roman Senate") sent the imperial insignia to the [Eastern Roman Emperor](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Emperor "Eastern Roman Emperor") [Zeno](/wiki/Zeno_(emperor) "Zeno (emperor)").

While its legitimacy lasted for centuries longer and its cultural influence remains today, the Western Empire never had the strength to rise again. The Eastern Roman, or [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), Empire, survived and remained for centuries an effective power of the [Eastern Mediterranean](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean "Eastern Mediterranean"), although it lessened in strength. While the loss of political unity and military control is universally acknowledged, the fall of Rome is not the only unifying concept for these events; the period described as [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity") emphasizes the cultural continuities throughout and beyond the political collapse.

## Historical approaches and modern syntheses

Main article: [Historiography of the fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Historiography_of_the_fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Historiography of the fall of the Western Roman Empire")

Since 1776, when [Edward Gibbon](/wiki/Edward_Gibbon "Edward Gibbon") published the first volume of his *[The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire](/wiki/The_History_of_the_Decline_and_Fall_of_the_Roman_Empire "The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire")*, Decline and Fall has been the theme around which much of the history of the Roman Empire has been structured. "From the eighteenth century onward," historian [Glen Bowersock](/wiki/Glen_Bowersock "Glen Bowersock") wrote, "we have been obsessed with the fall: it has been valued as an archetype for every perceived decline, and, hence, as a symbol for our own fears."[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Another paradigm of the period

Main article: [Late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity")

From at least the time of [Henri Pirenne](/wiki/Henri_Pirenne "Henri Pirenne") (1862–1935), scholars have described a continuity of [Roman culture](/wiki/Culture_of_ancient_Rome "Culture of ancient Rome") and political legitimacy long after 476.[[6]](#cite_note-Ramsay_MacMullen_1981-6): 5–7[[7]](#cite_note-survival_greece-7) Pirenne postponed the demise of [classical civilization](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") to the 8th century. He challenged the notion that [Germanic barbarians](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period") had caused the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") to end, and he refused to equate the end of the Western Roman Empire with the end of the office of [emperor](/wiki/Roman_Emperor "Roman Emperor") in Italy. He pointed out the essential continuity of the economy of the Roman [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") even after the [barbarian](/wiki/Barbarian "Barbarian") invasions, and suggested that only the [Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests") represented a decisive break with antiquity.

The more recent formulation of a historical period characterized as "[Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity")" emphasizes the transformations of ancient to medieval worlds within a cultural continuity.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown19782–3-8) In recent decades archaeologically based argument even extends the continuity in [material culture](/wiki/Material_culture "Material culture") and in patterns of settlement as late as the eleventh century.[[9]](#cite_note-Lavan-9)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunt2001256-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Anna_Leone-12) Observing the political reality of lost control (and the attendant fragmentation of commerce, culture, and language), but also the cultural and archaeological continuities, the process has been described as a complex cultural transformation, rather than a fall.[[13]](#cite_note-Bowersock-13): 34 "The perception of Late Antiquity has significantly changed: the period is no longer seen as an era of decline and crisis but as an epoch of metamorphosis in the Mediterranean region".[[14]](#cite_note-Rebenich_in_Rousseau-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Johnson_2015-15): 3, 4

### Major phases

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Invasions_of_the_Roman_Empire_1.png/250px-Invasions_of_the_Roman_Empire_1.png)](/wiki/File:Invasions_of_the_Roman_Empire_1.png)

Routes taken by barbarian invaders of the Roman Empire during the [Migration Period](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period")

A synthesis by Harper (2017) gave four decisive turns of events in the transformation from the height of the empire to the early Middle Ages:

* The [Antonine Plague](/wiki/Antonine_Plague "Antonine Plague") that ended a long period of demographic and economic expansion, weakening but not toppling the empire.
* The [Crisis of the Third Century](/wiki/Crisis_of_the_Third_Century "Crisis of the Third Century"), in which natural [climate change](/wiki/Climate_variability_and_change "Climate variability and change"), renewed [pandemic disease](/wiki/Plague_of_Cyprian "Plague of Cyprian"), and internal and external political instability led to the near-collapse of the imperial system. Its reconstitution included a new basis for the currency, an expanded professional government apparatus, emperors further distanced from their people, and, shortly, the rise of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), a proselytizing, [exclusive religion](/wiki/Religious_exclusivism "Religious exclusivism") that anticipated the [imminent end of the world](/wiki/Eschatology "Eschatology").
* The military and political failure of the West, in which mass migration from the [Eurasian steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_steppe "Eurasian steppe") overcame and dismembered the western part of an internally weakened empire. The eastern empire rebuilt itself again and began the reconquest of the West.
* In the lands around the Mediterranean the [Late Antique Little Ice Age](/wiki/Late_Antique_Little_Ice_Age "Late Antique Little Ice Age") and the [Plague of Justinian](/wiki/Plague_of_Justinian "Plague of Justinian") created one of the worst environmental cataclysms in recorded history. The imperial system crumbled in the next couple of generations and then lost vast territories to the armies of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), a new proselytizing, exclusive religion that also looked forward to an imminent end time. The diminished and impoverished Byzantine rump state survived amid perpetual strife between and among the followers of Christianity and Islam.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper201721-16)

The loss of centralized political control over the West, and the lessened power of the East, are universally agreed, but the theme of decline has been taken to cover a much wider time span than the hundred years from 376. For [Cassius Dio](/wiki/Cassius_Dio "Cassius Dio"), the accession of the emperor [Commodus](/wiki/Commodus "Commodus") in 180 CE marked the descent "from a kingdom of gold to one of rust and iron".[[17]](#cite_note-17) Since the age of [humanism](/wiki/Humanism "Humanism"), the process of the Fall has been thought to have begun with [Constantine the Great](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great"), or with the [soldier emperors](/wiki/Barracks_emperors "Barracks emperors") who seized power through command of the army from 235 through 284, or with [Commodus](/wiki/Commodus "Commodus"), or even with [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus").[[14]](#cite_note-Rebenich_in_Rousseau-14)

Gibbon was uncertain about when decline began. "In the first paragraph of his text, Gibbon wrote that he intended to trace the decline from the golden age of the Antonines"; later text has it beginning about A.D. 180 with the death of Marcus Aurelius; while in chapter 7, he pushes the start of the decline to about 52 B.C., the time of Julius Caesar and Pompey and Cicero.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGruman196084–85-18) Gibbon placed the western empire's end with the removal of the man Gibbon referred to as "the helpless Augustulus" in 476.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBowersock199631-19)

[Arnold J. Toynbee](/wiki/Arnold_J._Toynbee "Arnold J. Toynbee") and [James Burke](/wiki/James_Burke_(science_historian) "James Burke (science historian)") argue that the entire Imperial era was one of steady decay of institutions founded in [republican](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") times. [Theodor Mommsen](/wiki/Theodor_Mommsen "Theodor Mommsen") excluded the imperial period from his Nobel Prize-winning *[History of Rome](/wiki/History_of_Rome_(Mommsen) "History of Rome (Mommsen)")* (1854–1856). As one convenient marker for the end, 476 has been used since Gibbon, but other key dates for the fall of the Roman Empire in the West include the Crisis of the Third Century, the [Crossing of the Rhine](/wiki/Crossing_of_the_Rhine "Crossing of the Rhine") in 406 (or 405), the [sack of Rome in 410](/wiki/Sack_of_Rome_(410) "Sack of Rome (410)"), and the death of [Julius Nepos](/wiki/Julius_Nepos "Julius Nepos") in 480.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMomigliano1973-20)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Causes, according to Gibbon (1776–1789)

When Gibbon published his landmark work, it quickly became the standard text.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJordan196983,_93–94-21)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibbon1906279,_312-22) Peter Brown has written that "Gibbon's work formed the peak of a century of scholarship which had been conducted in the belief that the study of the declining Roman Empire was also the study of the origins of modern Europe".[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown201337-23) Gibbon was the first to attempt an explanation of causes of a fall of empire.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown201337-23) Like other [Enlightenment](/wiki/The_Age_of_Enlightenment "The Age of Enlightenment") thinkers and [British](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") citizens of the age steeped in institutional [anti-Catholicism](/wiki/Anti-Catholicism "Anti-Catholicism"), Gibbon held in contempt the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Medievalism "Medievalism") as a priest-ridden, superstitious Dark Age. It was not until his own era, the "Age of Reason", with its emphasis on rational thought, it was believed, that human history could resume its progress.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

He began an ongoing controversy about the role of Christianity, but he gave great weight to other causes of internal decline and to [attacks from outside the Empire](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period").

> The story of its ruin is simple and obvious; and, instead of inquiring why the Roman empire was destroyed, we should rather be surprised that it had subsisted so long. The victorious legions, who, in distant wars, acquired the vices of strangers and mercenaries, first oppressed the freedom of the republic, and afterwards violated the majesty of the purple. The emperors, anxious for their personal safety and the public peace, were reduced to the base expedient of corrupting the discipline which rendered them alike formidable to their sovereign and to the enemy; the vigour of the military government was relaxed, and finally dissolved, by the partial institutions of Constantine; and the Roman world was overwhelmed by a deluge of Barbarians.

— Edward Gibbon. *The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire*, Chapter 38 "General Observations on the Fall of the Roman Empire in the West"

> After a diligent inquiry, I can discern four principal causes of the ruin of Rome, which continued to operate in a period of more than a thousand years. I. The injuries of time and nature. II. The hostile attacks of the Barbarians and Christians. III. The use and abuse of the materials. And, IV. The domestic quarrels of the Romans.

— Edward Gibbon. *The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire*, Chapter 71 "Four Causes of Decay and Destruction."

### Contemporary scholarship

Modern historiography diverges from Gibbon.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndo201260-26) While most of his ideas are no longer accepted in totality, they have been foundational to later discourse and the modern synthesis with archaeology, epidemiology, climatic history, genetic science,[[27]](#cite_note-27) and many more new sources of history beyond the documentary sources which were all that was available to Gibbon.[[14]](#cite_note-Rebenich_in_Rousseau-14)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

While [Alexander Demandt](/wiki/Alexander_Demandt "Alexander Demandt") enumerated 210 different theories on why Rome fell,[[29]](#cite_note-29) twenty-first century scholarship classifies the primary possibilities more concisely:[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalinsky199253–73-30)

#### Climatic crisis

A recent summary interprets disease and [climate change](/wiki/Climate_variability_and_change "Climate variability and change") as important drivers of the political collapse of the empire. There was a [Roman climatic optimum](/wiki/Roman_Warm_Period "Roman Warm Period") from about 200 BCE to 150 CE, when lands around the Mediterranean were generally warm and well-watered. This made agriculture prosperous, army recruitment easy, and the collection of taxes straightforward. From about 150, the climate became on average somewhat worse for most of the inhabited lands around the Mediterranean.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017chapter_4_"The_Old_Age_of_the_World-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) After about 450, the climate worsened further in the [Late Antique Little Ice Age](/wiki/Late_Antique_Little_Ice_Age "Late Antique Little Ice Age") that may have directly contributed to the variety of factors that caused Rome to collapse.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017248–254-33)

The Roman Empire was built on the fringes of the [tropics](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics"). Its roads and seas, which produced an abundance of trade, also unknowingly created an interconnected [disease ecology](/wiki/Disease_ecology "Disease ecology") that unleashed the evolution and spread of pathogens.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper20175-34) [Pandemics](/wiki/Pandemic "Pandemic") contributed to massive demographic changes, [economic crises](/wiki/Economic_crises "Economic crises"), and [food shortages](/wiki/Famine "Famine") in the [crisis of the third century](/wiki/Crisis_of_the_third_century "Crisis of the third century").[[35]](#cite_note-Turchin_and_Scheidel-35)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017112–113-36)[[37]](#cite_note-Walter_Scheidel-37) Heavy mortality in 165–180 from the [Antonine Plague](/wiki/Antonine_Plague "Antonine Plague") seriously impaired attempts to repel [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") invaders, but the legions generally held or at least speedily re-instated the borders of the Empire.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibbon1782Chapter_I:_The_Extent_Of_The_Empire_In_The_Age_Of_The_Antonines._Chapter_II:_The_Internal_Prosperity_In_The_Age_Of_The_Antonines._Chapter_III:_The_Constitution_In_The_Age_Of_The_Antonines.-38)

[![Map of the Roman Empire in the early second century](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/RomanEmpire_117.svg/500px-RomanEmpire_117.svg.png)](/wiki/File:RomanEmpire_117.svg)

Roman Empire in the early second century

#### Migrational crisis

From 376, [massive populations moved](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period") into the Empire, driven by the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns") who themselves may have been driven by climate change in the [Eurasian steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_steppe "Eurasian steppe").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017-1)[[39]](#cite_note-39) These barbarian invasions led ultimately to [barbarian kingdoms](/wiki/Barbarian_kingdoms "Barbarian kingdoms") over much of the former territory of the Western Empire. But the final blow came only with the [Late Antique Little Ice Age](/wiki/Late_Antique_Little_Ice_Age "Late Antique Little Ice Age") and its aftermath,[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017248–254-33) when Rome was already politically fragmented and materially depleted.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017264–267-40)

#### Political crisis

[Aurelian](/wiki/Aurelian "Aurelian") reunited the empire in 274, and from 284 [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian") and his successors reorganized it with more emphasis on the military. [John the Lydian](/wiki/John_the_Lydian "John the Lydian"), writing over two centuries later, reported that Diocletian's army at one point totaled 389,704 men, plus 45,562 in the fleets, and numbers may have increased later.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather200663–64-41) With the limited communications of the time, both the European and the Eastern frontiers needed the attention of their own [supreme commanders](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief"). Diocletian tried to solve this problem by re-establishing an adoptive succession with a senior (*[Augustus](/wiki/Augustus_(honorific) "Augustus (honorific)")*) and junior (*[Caesar](/wiki/Caesar_(title) "Caesar (title)")*) emperor in each half of the Empire, but this system of [tetrarchy](/wiki/Tetrarchy "Tetrarchy") broke down within one generation, and the biological families of emperors again became the expected successors to the throne, with generally unfortunate results. Thereafter [civil war](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war") was the main method of establishing new imperial [regimes](/wiki/Regime "Regime"). Although [Constantine the Great](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") (in office 306 to 337) again re-united the Empire, his sons' generation fought destructive civil wars between themselves and with usurpers. Towards the end of the fourth century the need for division was generally accepted. From then on, the Empire existed in constant tension between the need for two emperors and their mutual [mistrust](/wiki/Distrust "Distrust").[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather200667-42)

Until late in the fourth century, the united Empire retained sufficient power to launch powerful attacks against its enemies in [Germania](/wiki/Germania "Germania") and in the [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"). *Receptio* of barbarians became widely practised: imperial authorities admitted potentially hostile groups into the Empire, split them up, and allotted to them lands, status, and duties within the imperial system.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In this way many groups provided [unfree workers](/wiki/Colonus_(person) "Colonus (person)") for Roman landowners, and [recruits ("laeti")](/wiki/Laeti "Laeti") for the [Roman army](/wiki/Roman_army "Roman army"). Sometimes their leaders became military officers or civil office holders. Normally, the Romans managed the process carefully, with sufficient military force on hand to ensure compliance. [Cultural assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation"), into the ways of Rome and the expectations of its citizenry, followed—typically over the next generation or two.

[![Map of the Roman Empire under the Tetrarchy, showing the dioceses and the four Tetrarchs' zones of influence](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Tetrarchy_map3.jpg/250px-Tetrarchy_map3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tetrarchy_map3.jpg)

The Roman Empire under the Tetrarchy, showing the dioceses and the four Tetrarchs' zones of responsibility

#### Financial crisis

Main article: [Crisis of the Third Century](/wiki/Crisis_of_the_Third_Century "Crisis of the Third Century")

The Empire suffered multiple serious crises during the third century. The rising [Sassanid Empire](/wiki/Sassanid_Empire "Sassanid Empire") inflicted three crushing defeats on Roman [field armies](/wiki/Field_army "Field army") and remained a potent threat for centuries.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather200667-42) Other disasters included [repeated civil wars](/wiki/Roman_Emperor_(Crisis_of_the_Third_Century) "Roman Emperor (Crisis of the Third Century)"), barbarian invasions, and more mass-mortality in the [Plague of Cyprian](/wiki/Plague_of_Cyprian "Plague of Cyprian") (from 250 onwards). For a short period, the Empire split into a [Gallic Empire](/wiki/Gallic_Empire "Gallic Empire") in the West (260–274), a [Palmyrene Empire](/wiki/Palmyrene_Empire "Palmyrene Empire") in the East (260–273), and a central Roman [rump state](/wiki/Rump_state "Rump state"); in 271, Rome abandoned the province of [Dacia](/wiki/Roman_Dacia "Roman Dacia") on the north of the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"). The [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine")/Danube frontier also came under more effective threats from larger barbarian groupings, which had developed improved agriculture and increased their populations.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006123-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) The average stature of the population in the West suffered a serious decline in the late second century; the population of [Northwestern Europe](/wiki/Northwestern_Europe "Northwestern Europe") did not recover, though the Mediterranean regions did.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The Empire survived the "Crisis of the Third Century", directing its economy successfully towards defense, but survival came at the price of [a more centralized and bureaucratic state](/wiki/Dominate "Dominate"). Excessive military expenditure, coupled with civil wars due to unstable succession, caused increased taxes to the detriment of the industry.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Under [Gallienus](/wiki/Gallienus "Gallienus") (Emperor from 253 to 268) the senatorial aristocracy ceased joining the ranks of the senior military commanders. Its typical members lacked interest in military service, and showed incompetence at command.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELetki201252–53-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[![The divided Empire in 271 CE](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Map_of_Ancient_Rome_271_AD.svg/250px-Map_of_Ancient_Rome_271_AD.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Ancient_Rome_271_AD.svg)

The divided Empire in 271 CE

Under Constantine, the cities lost their revenue from local taxes, and under [Constantius II](/wiki/Constantius_II "Constantius II") (r. 337–361) their endowments of property.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964131-50) This worsened the existing difficulty in keeping the city councils up to strength, and the services provided by the cities were scamped or abandoned.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964131-50) Public building projects had declined since the second century. There is no evidence of state participation in, or support for, restoration and maintenance of [temples](/wiki/Temple "Temple") and [shrines](/wiki/Shrine "Shrine"); rather, restorations had to be funded and accomplished privately, which limited what was done.[[12]](#cite_note-Anna_Leone-12): 36–39 A further financial abuse was Constantius's habit of granting to his immediate entourage the estates of persons condemned for [treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason") and other [capital crimes](/wiki/Capital_punishment#Capital_crime "Capital punishment"). This practice reduced future, though not immediate, income; those close to the emperor also gained a strong incentive to encourage his suspicion of [conspiracies](/wiki/Conspiracy "Conspiracy").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964131-50)

Corruption, in this context the diversion of finance from the needs of the army, may have contributed greatly to the Fall. The rich senatorial aristocrats in Rome itself became increasingly influential during the fifth century; they supported armed strength in theory, but did not wish to pay for it or to offer their own workers as army recruits.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlföldy200117-51)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacgeorge2002171–172-52) They did, however, pass large amounts of money to the Christian Church.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacgeorge2002172-53)

#### Social crisis

The new supreme rulers disposed of the [legal fiction](/wiki/Legal_fiction "Legal fiction") of the [early Empire](/wiki/Principate "Principate") (seeing the emperor as but the [first among equals](/wiki/Princeps "Princeps")); emperors from Aurelian (r. 270–275) onwards openly styled themselves as *[dominus](/wiki/Dominus_(title) "Dominus (title)") et [deus](/wiki/Deus "Deus")*, "lord and god", titles appropriate for a master-slave relationship.[[54]](#cite_note-54) An elaborate court [ceremonial](/wiki/Ceremony "Ceremony") developed, and obsequious [flattery](/wiki/Flattery "Flattery") became the order of the day. Under Diocletian, the flow of direct requests to the emperor rapidly reduced, and soon ceased altogether. No other form of direct access replaced them, and the emperor received only information filtered through his [courtiers](/wiki/Courtier "Courtier").[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988110,_147-55) However, as [Sabine MacCormack](/wiki/Sabine_G._MacCormack "Sabine G. MacCormack") described, the court culture that developed with Diocletian was still subject to pressure from below. Imperial proclamations were used to stress the traditional limitations of the imperial office, while imperial ceremonies "left room for consensus and popular participation".[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown201348-56)

Official [cruelty](/wiki/Cruelty "Cruelty"), supporting [extortion](/wiki/Extortion "Extortion") and [corruption](/wiki/Corruption "Corruption"), may also have become more commonplace;[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988137–142-57) one example being [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great")'s law that slaves who betrayed their mistress's confidential remarks should have molten lead poured down their throats.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) While the scale, complexity, and violence of government were unmatched,[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews2007253-60) the emperors lost control over their whole realm insofar as that control came increasingly to be wielded by [anyone who paid for it](/wiki/Kleptocracy "Kleptocracy").[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988170-61) Meanwhile, the richest senatorial families, immune from most taxation, engrossed more and more of the available wealth and income[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameron201197-62)[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews2007278-63) while also becoming divorced from any tradition of military excellence. At a local level, from the early fourth century, the town councils lost their property and their power, which often became concentrated in the hands of a few local despots beyond the reach of the law.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988-64) One scholar identifies a great increase in the purchasing power of gold, two and a half fold from 274 to the later fourth century. This may be an index of growing [economic inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality") between a gold-rich elite and a cash-poor [peasantry](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERathbone2009324-65) "Formerly, says Ammianus, Rome was saved by her austerity, by solidarity between rich and poor, by contempt for death; now she is undone by her luxury and greed (Amm. xxxi. 5. 14 and xxii. 4.). Salvianus backs up Ammianus by affirming that greed (avaritia) is a vice common to nearly all Romans".[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiganiol195066–67-66)[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] However, [Lucius Calpurnius Piso Frugi (consul 133 BC)](/wiki/Lucius_Calpurnius_Piso_Frugi_(consul_133_BC) "Lucius Calpurnius Piso Frugi (consul 133 BC)") had already dated the start of Rome's moral decline to 154 BCE.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

Within the [late Roman military](/wiki/Late_Roman_army "Late Roman army"), many recruits and even officers had barbarian origins. Soldiers are recorded as using possibly-barbarian rituals, such as elevating a claimant on shields.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews2007284-68) Some scholars have seen this as an indication of weakness. Others disagree, seeing neither barbarian recruits nor new rituals as causing any problem with the effectiveness or loyalty of the army, at least while that army was effectively led, disciplined, trained, paid, and supplied by officers who identified as Roman.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006119-69)

#### Geography

[A. H. M. Jones](/wiki/A._H._M._Jones "A. H. M. Jones") has pointed out that the earlier scholarly views are Western.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones19641026-70) Most of the weaknesses discussed by scholars were "common to both halves of the empire", with Christianity even more prevalent in the East than the West. Religious disputes were bitter, [bureaucracy](/wiki/Bureaucracy "Bureaucracy") corrupt and extortionate, it had a [caste system](/wiki/Caste_system "Caste system"), and land fell out of use in the East just as it had in the West.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones19641027-71) Yet the East stood its ground in the fifth century, fought back in the sixth, and even recovered some territory in the seventh. The East had only one apparent advantage: geography. It was less vulnerable, strategically, than the West. The narrowest sea crossing to its core territories was protected from the northern barbarians by the fortifications and the sea and land forces of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), while the European frontier from the mouth of the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") to that of the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") is some 2000 kilometres [great-circle distance](/wiki/Great-circle_distance "Great-circle distance") and could be crossed with much less difficulty.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones19641027–1028-72) "The devastations of the barbarians impoverished and depopulated the [Western] frontier provinces, and their unceasing pressure imposed on the empire a burden of defense which overstrained its administrative machinery and its economic resources... [playing] a major part in the fall of the West".[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones19641027-71)

## Height of power, systematic weaknesses as direct causes

The Roman Empire reached its greatest geographical extent under [Trajan](/wiki/Trajan "Trajan") (r. 98–117), who ruled a prosperous state that stretched from [Armenia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Armenia_(antiquity) "Kingdom of Armenia (antiquity)") to the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). The Empire had large numbers of trained, supplied, and disciplined soldiers, drawn from a growing population. It had a comprehensive [civil administration](/wiki/Civil_administration "Civil administration") based in thriving cities with effective control over public finances. The literate [elite](/wiki/Elite "Elite") considered theirs to be the only worthwhile form of civilization, giving the Empire ideological legitimacy and a cultural unity based on comprehensive familiarity with [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_literature "Ancient Greek literature") and [Roman literature](/wiki/Latin_literature "Latin literature") and [rhetoric](/wiki/Rhetoric "Rhetoric"). The Empire's power allowed it to maintain extreme differences of wealth and status.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiganiol195066-73) Its wide-ranging [trade networks](/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route") permitted even modest households to use goods made by [professionals](/wiki/Professional "Professional") far away.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard-Perkins200587–121-74)

The empire had both strength and resilience. Its financial system allowed it to raise significant taxes which, despite endemic corruption, supported a large [regular army](/wiki/Imperial_Roman_army "Imperial Roman army") with logistics and training. The *[cursus honorum](/wiki/Cursus_honorum "Cursus honorum")*, a standardized series of military and civil posts organised for ambitious aristocratic men, ensured that powerful noblemen had the opportunity to become familiar with military and civil command and administration. At a lower level within the army, connecting the aristocrats at the top with the private soldiers, a large number of [centurions](/wiki/Centurion "Centurion") were well-rewarded, literate, and responsible for training, discipline, administration, and [leadership](/wiki/Leadership "Leadership") in battle.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldsworthy200368–73-75) City governments with their own properties and revenues functioned effectively at a local level; [membership of city councils](/wiki/Curiales "Curiales") involved lucrative opportunities for independent decision-making, and, despite its obligations, became seen as a privilege. Under a [series of emperors](/wiki/Nerva%E2%80%93Antonine_dynasty "Nerva–Antonine dynasty") who each adopted a mature and capable successor, the Empire did not require civil wars to regulate the imperial succession. Requests could be submitted directly to the better emperors, and the answers had the force of law, putting the imperial power directly in touch with even humble subjects.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988110-76) The [cults](/wiki/Cult_(religious_practice) "Cult (religious practice)") of [polytheist](/wiki/Polytheism "Polytheism") religion were hugely varied, but none claimed that theirs was the only truth. Their followers displayed mutual [tolerance](/wiki/Religious_toleration "Religious toleration"), producing a polyphonous religious harmony.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Religious strife was rare after the suppression of the [Bar Kokhba revolt](/wiki/Bar_Kokhba_revolt "Bar Kokhba revolt") in 136, after which the devastated [Judaea](/wiki/Judaea_(Roman_province) "Judaea (Roman province)") ceased to be a major centre for Jewish unrest.

Nevertheless, it remained a culture based on an early [subsistence economy](/wiki/Subsistence_economy "Subsistence economy"), with only ineffective inklings of a [germ theory of disease](/wiki/Germ_theory_of_disease "Germ theory of disease"). Despite its [aqueducts](/wiki/Aqueduct_(water_supply) "Aqueduct (water supply)"), the [water supply](/wiki/Water_supply "Water supply") did not allow good hygiene. [Sewage](/wiki/Sewage "Sewage") was disposed of on the streets, in open drains, or by scavenging animals. Even in the [Roman Climatic Optimum](/wiki/Roman_Climatic_Optimum "Roman Climatic Optimum"), local harvest failures causing famines were always a possibility.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017-1)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] And even in good times, [Roman women](/wiki/Women_in_ancient_Rome "Women in ancient Rome") needed to have, on average, six children each in order to [maintain the population](/wiki/Demography_of_the_Roman_Empire "Demography of the Roman Empire").[[78]](#cite_note-78) Good nourishment and bodily cleanliness were privileges of the rich, advertised by their firm tread, healthy skin color, and lack of the "dull smell of the underbathed".[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2012220–221-79) [Infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") was very high, and [diarrhoeal](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea") diseases were a major cause of death. [Malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria") was endemic in many areas, notably in the city of [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") itself, possibly encouraged by the enthusiasm of rich Romans for water features in their gardens.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017-1)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Rise of Christianity, possible decline of the armed forces

Further information: [History of late ancient Christianity](/wiki/History_of_late_ancient_Christianity "History of late ancient Christianity")

See also: [Christianization of the Roman Empire as diffusion of innovation](/wiki/Christianization_of_the_Roman_Empire_as_diffusion_of_innovation "Christianization of the Roman Empire as diffusion of innovation")

In 313, [Constantine the Great](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") declared [official toleration of Christianity](/wiki/Edict_of_Milan "Edict of Milan"). This was followed over the ensuing decades by the search for a definition of [Christian orthodoxy](/wiki/Orthodoxy#Christianity "Orthodoxy") all could agree upon. Creeds were developed, but Christianity has never agreed upon an official version of its Bible or its doctrine; instead it has had many different manuscript traditions.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Christianity's disputes may have effected decline. Official and private action was taken against [heterodox](/wiki/Heterodoxy "Heterodoxy") Christians (heretics) from the fourth century up to the modern era. Limited action against [pagans](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism"), who were mostly ignored, was based on the contempt that accompanied Christianity's sense of triumph after Constantine.[[81]](#cite_note-CAH_1998-81) Christianity opposed sacrifice and magic, and Christian emperors made laws that favored Christianity. Constantine's successors generally continued this approach, and by the end of the fourth century, Christianity had become the religion of any ambitious civil official.

The wealth of the [Christian Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church") increased dramatically in the fifth century. Immense resources, both public and private, were used for building churches, storage barns for the grain used for charity, new hospitals for the poor, and in support of those in religious life without other income.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen198851-82) [Bishops](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") in wealthy cities were thus able to offer [patronage](/wiki/Patronage_in_ancient_Rome "Patronage in ancient Rome") in the long-established manner of Roman aristocrats. Ammianus described some who "enriched from the offerings of matrons, ride seated in carriages, wearing clothing chosen with care, and serve banquets so lavish that their entertainments outdo the tables of kings".

But the move to Christianity probably had no significant effects on public finances.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006123-44) The large temple complexes, with professional full-time priests, festivals, and large numbers of sacrifices (which became free food for the masses), had also been expensive to maintain. They had already been negatively impacted by the empire's financial struggles in the third century.[[83]](#cite_note-Scott_Bradbury-83): 353[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2003-84): 60 The numbers of [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy"), [monks](/wiki/Monk "Monk"), and [nuns](/wiki/Nun "Nun") increased to perhaps half the size of the actual army, and they have been considered as a drain on limited manpower.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarper2017186-86)

The numbers and effectiveness of the regular soldiers may have declined during the fourth century. [Payrolls](/wiki/Payroll "Payroll") were inflated, so that pay could be diverted and exemptions from duty sold. The soldiers' opportunities for personal extortion were multiplied by residence in cities, while their effectiveness was reduced by concentration on extortion instead of [military exercises](/wiki/Military_exercise "Military exercise").[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988175-87) However, [extortion](/wiki/Matthew_5:41 "Matthew 5:41"), [gross corruption](/wiki/Didius_Julianus#Rise_to_power "Didius Julianus"), and occasional ineffectiveness[[88]](#cite_note-88) were not new to the Roman army. There is no consensus whether its effectiveness significantly declined before 376.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicasie1998187-89) [Ammianus Marcellinus](/wiki/Ammianus_Marcellinus "Ammianus Marcellinus"), himself a professional soldier, repeats longstanding observations about the superiority of contemporary Roman armies being due to training and discipline, not to individual size or strength.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard-Perkins200537-90) He also accuses [Valentinian I](/wiki/Valentinian_I "Valentinian I") of being the first emperor to increase the arrogance of the military, raising their rank and power to excess, severely punishing the minor crimes of the common soldiers, while sparing those of higher rank who felt able to commit shameful and monstrous crimes.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Despite a possible decrease in the Empire's ability to assemble and supply large armies,[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988173–175,_181-92) Rome maintained an aggressive and potent stance against perceived threats almost to the end of the fourth century.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicasie1998261-93)

## The Tetrarchy to Valentinian I: civil and foreign wars (317-375)

[Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") settled [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") on the lower left bank of the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"). Their communities required a line of [fortifications](/wiki/Fortification "Fortification") to keep them in check, indicating that Rome had lost almost all local control.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988137–142-57) Under Constantius, [bandits](/wiki/Banditry "Banditry") came to dominate areas such as [Isauria](/wiki/Isauria "Isauria"), which were well within the empire.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988181–183-94) The tribes of Germania also became more populous and more threatening.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006123-44) In [Gaul](/wiki/Roman_Gaul "Roman Gaul"), which did not really recover from the [invasions of the third century](/wiki/Barbarian_invasions_into_the_Roman_Empire_of_the_3rd_century "Barbarian invasions into the Roman Empire of the 3rd century"), there was widespread insecurity and economic decline in the 300s,[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006123-44) perhaps worst in [Armorica](/wiki/Armorica "Armorica"). By 350, after decades of [pirate](/wiki/Piracy "Piracy") attacks, virtually all [villas](/wiki/Roman_villa "Roman villa") in Armorica were deserted. Local use of money ceased around 360.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen198823,_178,_186-95) Repeated attempts to economize on military expenditure included billeting troops in cities, where they could less easily be kept under military discipline and could more easily extort from civilians.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988161-96) Except in the rare case of a determined and incorruptible general, these troops proved ineffective in action and dangerous to civilians.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988190–193-97) [Frontier troops](/wiki/Limitanei "Limitanei") were often given land rather than pay. As they farmed for themselves, their direct costs diminished, but so did their effectiveness, and their pay gave much less stimulus to the frontier economy.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988176-98) However, except for the provinces along the lower Rhine, the agricultural economy was generally doing well.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006112–115-99)

On January 18 350, the imperial [magister officiorum](/wiki/Marcellinus_(magister_officiorum) "Marcellinus (magister officiorum)") gave a banquet in [Augustodunum](/wiki/Augustodunum "Augustodunum") while his master, Western Emperor [Constans](/wiki/Constans "Constans"), was away hunting. During the feast [Magnus Magnentius](/wiki/Magnentius "Magnentius"), commander of the [imperial household troops](/wiki/Jovians_and_Herculians "Jovians and Herculians"), appeared in an imperial purple toga and announced himself to be the new Emperor. Constans was soon murdered and Magnentius took over most of his western domains. He made peace overtures to [Constantius](/wiki/Constantius_II "Constantius II") in the East, but these failed. In the ensuing [bloody civil war](/wiki/Roman_civil_war_of_350%E2%80%93353 "Roman civil war of 350–353") Magnentius marched against Constantius with as many troops as he could mobilize, stripping the Rhine frontier of its most effective troops. Magnentius died and so did many of his men. Meanwhile, Constantius sent messages to the German tribes east of the Rhine, inviting them to attack Gaul, which they did. In the next few years a strip some 64 kilometres (40 mi) wide to the west of the Rhine was occupied by the Germans, and a further 190 kilometres (120 mi) into Gaul the surviving population and garrisons had fled.[[100]](#cite_note-David_Wigg-Wolf-100)

[![Solidus, obverse showing Julian as philosopher, reverse symbolizing the strength of the Roman army](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/13/Solidus_Julian-transparent.png)](/wiki/File:Solidus_Julian-transparent.png)

[Solidus](/wiki/Solidus_(coin) "Solidus (coin)") of Julian, *c.* 361. Obverse: Julian with the beard appropriate to a [Neoplatonic](/wiki/Neoplatonic "Neoplatonic") philosopher. Inscription: FL(AVIVS) CL(AVDIVS) IVLIANVS PP(=[Pater Patriae](/wiki/Pater_Patriae "Pater Patriae"), "father of the nation") AVG(=Augustus). Reverse: an armed Roman, military standard in one hand, a captive in the other. Inscription: VIRTVS EXERCITVS ROMANORVM, "the bravery/virtue of the Roman army"; the mint mark is SIRM, [Sirmium](/wiki/Sirmium "Sirmium").

[Julian](/wiki/Julian_(emperor) "Julian (emperor)") (r. 360–363) won [victories against Germans](/wiki/Battle_of_Strasbourg "Battle of Strasbourg") who had invaded Gaul. He launched a drive against official corruption, which allowed the tax demands in Gaul to be reduced to one-third of their previous amount, while all government requirements were still met.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmmianus1935book_XVI,_chapter_V-101) In civil legislation, Julian was notable for his pro-pagan policies. Julian lifted the ban on [sacrifices](/wiki/Sacrifice "Sacrifice"), restored and reopened temples, and dismantled the privileged tax status and revenue concessions of the Christians. He gave generous tax remissions to the cities which he favored, and disfavor to those who remained Christian.[[102]](#cite_note-Julian_CAH-102): 62–65[[103]](#cite_note-103) Julian ordered toleration of varieties of Christianity banned as heretical by Constantius;[[102]](#cite_note-Julian_CAH-102) possibly, he would not have been able to persecute effectively such a large and powerful group as Christians had now become.: 62[[104]](#cite_note-Scott_Bradbury_1995-104): 345–346[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis200594–95-105): 62

Julian prepared for civil war against Constantius, who again encouraged the Germans to attack Gaul. However Julian's campaigns had been effective and only one small Alemannic raid, speedily dealt with by Julian, resulted.[[100]](#cite_note-David_Wigg-Wolf-100) Constantius died before any serious fighting and Julian was acknowledged as master of the entire Empire. He launched an expensive [campaign against the Sasanian Persians](/wiki/Julian%27s_Persian_expedition "Julian's Persian expedition").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964131-50) He succeeded in marching to the Sassanid capital of [Ctesiphon](/wiki/Ctesiphon "Ctesiphon"), but, at the suggestion of a Persian agent, burned his boats and supplies to show resolve in continuing operations. The Sassanids then [burned crops](/wiki/Scorched_earth "Scorched earth") so the Roman army had no food. Finding himself cut off without supplies in enemy territory, Julian began a land retreat, and during the [Battle of Samarra](/wiki/Battle_of_Samarra "Battle of Samarra"), he was mortally wounded.[[106]](#cite_note-Ancient_Rome-106)[[102]](#cite_note-Julian_CAH-102): 74

Julian's successor [Jovian](/wiki/Jovian_(emperor) "Jovian (emperor)"), acclaimed by a demoralized army, began his brief reign (363–364) while trapped in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") without supplies. To purchase safe passage home, he had to concede areas of [northern Mesopotamia](/wiki/Upper_Mesopotamia "Upper Mesopotamia"), including the strategically important fortress of [Nisibis](/wiki/Nusaybin "Nusaybin"). This fortress had been Roman since before the [Peace of Nisibis](/wiki/Peace_of_Nisibis_(299) "Peace of Nisibis (299)") in 299.[[106]](#cite_note-Ancient_Rome-106)

The brothers [Valens](/wiki/Valens "Valens") (r. 364–378) and [Valentinian I](/wiki/Valentinian_I "Valentinian I") (r. 364–375) energetically tackled the threats of [barbarian attacks](/wiki/Great_Conspiracy "Great Conspiracy") on all the Western frontiers.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994283-107) They also tried to alleviate the burdens of taxation, which had risen continuously over the previous forty years; Valens in the East reduced the tax demand by half in his fourth year.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964147-108) Both of them were Christians, and re-confiscated the temple lands which Julian had restored. But they were generally tolerant of other beliefs. Valentinian in the West refused to intervene in Christian controversy. In the East, Valens had to deal with Christians who did not conform to his ideas of orthodoxy, and persecution formed part of his response. He tolerated paganism, even keeping some of Julian's associates in their trusted positions. He confirmed the rights and privileges of the pagan priests, and confirmed the right of pagans to be the exclusive caretakers of their temples.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones196426,_152-109)

Valentinian died of an [apoplexy](/wiki/Apoplexy "Apoplexy") while shouting at envoys of Germanic leaders. His successors in the West were children, his sons [Gratian](/wiki/Gratian "Gratian") (r. 375–383) and [Valentinian II](/wiki/Valentinian_II "Valentinian II") (r. 375–392). Gratian, "alien from the art of government both by temperament and by training", removed the [Altar of Victory](/wiki/Altar_of_Victory "Altar of Victory") from the [Senate House](/wiki/Curia_Julia "Curia Julia"). He also rejected the pagan title of [Pontifex Maximus](/wiki/Pontifex_Maximus "Pontifex Maximus").[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibbon1782Chapter_XXVII:_Civil_Wars,_Reign_Of_Theodosius._Part_I._Death_Of_Gratian.-110)

## Under Theodosius I and Valentinian II: invasions, civil wars, and religious discord (376–395)

### Battle of Adrianople

In 376, the East faced an enormous [barbarian influx](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period") across the Danube, mostly [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths"), who were fleeing from the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns"). They were exploited by corrupt officials rather than effectively relieved and resettled, and they took up arms and were joined by more Goths and some [Alans](/wiki/Alans "Alans") and Huns. Valens was in Asia with his main field army preparing for an assault on the Sasanian Empire. Redirection of the army and its logistic support would have required time, and Gratian's armies were distracted by Germanic invasions across the Rhine. In 378, Valens attacked the invaders with the Eastern field army, now perhaps 20,000 men, probably much fewer than the forces that Julian had led into Mesopotamia a little over a decade before, and possibly only 10% of the soldiers nominally available in the Danube provinces.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988185-111) In the [Battle of Adrianople](/wiki/Battle_of_Adrianople "Battle of Adrianople") (9 August 378), Valens lost much of that army and his own life. All of the [Balkan](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") provinces were thus exposed to raiding, without effective response from the remaining garrisons who were "more easily slaughtered than sheep".[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988185-111) Cities were able to hold their own [defensive walls](/wiki/Defensive_wall "Defensive wall") against barbarians who had no [siege equipment](/wiki/Siege_engine "Siege engine"), therefore the cities generally remained intact, although the [countryside](/wiki/Rural_area "Rural area") suffered.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicasie1998263ff-112)

### Partial recovery in the Balkans, internal corruption and financial desperation

Gratian appointed a new *Augustus*, a proven general from [Hispania](/wiki/Hispania "Hispania") called [Theodosius](/wiki/Theodosius_I "Theodosius I"). During the next four years, he partially re-established the Roman position in the East.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicasie1998256-113)[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007183-114) These campaigns depended on effective imperial coordination and mutual trust—between 379 and 380, Theodosius controlled not only the Eastern empire, but also, by agreement, the [diocese of Illyricum](/wiki/Diocese_of_Illyricum "Diocese of Illyricum").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns199448-115) Theodosius was unable to recruit enough Roman troops, relying on [barbarian warbands](/wiki/Foederati "Foederati") without Roman military discipline or loyalty. (In contrast, during the [Cimbrian War](/wiki/Cimbrian_War "Cimbrian War"), the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), controlling a smaller area than the western Empire, had been able to reconstitute large regular armies of citizens after greater defeats than Adrianople. That war had ended with the near-extermination of the invading barbarian supergroups, each supposed to have more than 100,000 warriors.[[116]](#cite_note-116))

The final Gothic settlement was acclaimed with relief,[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007183-114) even the official [panegyrist](/wiki/Panegyrist "Panegyrist") admitting that these Goths could not be expelled or exterminated, nor reduced to unfree status.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006188-117) Instead they were either recruited into the imperial forces, or settled in the devastated provinces along the south bank of the Danube, where the regular garrisons were never fully re-established.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns199454-118) In some later accounts, and widely in recent work, this is regarded as a treaty settlement, the first time that barbarians were given a home within the Empire, in which they retained their political and military cohesion.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964157-119) No formal treaty is recorded, nor details of whatever agreement was actually made. When the Goths are next mentioned in Roman records, they have different leaders and are soldiers of a sort.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007185-120) In 391, [Alaric](/wiki/Alaric_I "Alaric I"), a Gothic leader, rebelled against Roman control. Goths attacked the emperor himself, but within a year Alaric was accepted as a leader of Theodosius's Gothic troops and this rebellion was over.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994102,_152-121)

Theodosius's financial position must have been difficult, since he had to pay for expensive campaigning from a reduced tax base. The business of subduing barbarian warbands also demanded substantial gifts of precious metal.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns199465-122) At least one extra levy provoked desperation and [rioting](/wiki/Riot "Riot"), in which the emperor's statues were destroyed.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964162-123) Nevertheless, he is represented as financially generous as emperor, though frugal in his personal life.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964162,_169-124) By the end of the 380s, Theodosius and the court were in [Mediolanum](/wiki/Mediolanum "Mediolanum"), and [northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy") was experiencing a period of prosperity for the great landowners who took advantage of the court's need for food, "turning agrarian produce into gold", while repressing and misusing the poor who grew it and brought it in.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2012135-125) [Paulinus the Deacon](/wiki/Paulinus_the_Deacon "Paulinus the Deacon"), [notary](/wiki/Notary "Notary") of [Ambrose](/wiki/Ambrose "Ambrose") the [bishop of Milan](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Milan "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Milan"), described these men as creating a court where "everything was up for sale".[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2012136,_146-126) [Ambrose](/wiki/Ambrose "Ambrose") himself preached a series of sermons aimed at his wealthy constituents, asserting that avarice leads to a breakdown in society.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2012147-127)

For centuries, Theodosius was regarded as a champion of Christian orthodoxy who decisively stamped out paganism. His predecessors [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great"), [Constantius II](/wiki/Constantius_II "Constantius II"), and [Valens](/wiki/Valens "Valens") had all been [semi-Arians](/wiki/Semi-Arian "Semi-Arian"), whereas Theodosius supported [Nicene Christianity](/wiki/Nicene_Christianity "Nicene Christianity") which eventually became the orthodox version of [Christology](/wiki/Christology "Christology") for most later Christian churches—his [Edict of Thessalonica](/wiki/Edict_of_Thessalonica "Edict of Thessalonica") described Arian Christians as "foolish madmen". Therefore, as far as Ambrose and the Christian literary tradition that followed him were concerned, Theodosius deserved most of the credit for the final triumph of Christianity.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameron201074_(and_note_177)-128) Modern scholars see this as a Christian interpretation of history.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErrington2006248–249-130)[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameron201074-131)[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHebblewhite2020chapter_8-132) Theodosius did not stamp out paganism, which continued into the seventh century.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErrington2006248–249-130)[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameron201174-133)[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHebblewhite2020chapter_8-132)[[a]](#cite_note-140)

### Civil wars

Theodosius had to face a powerful usurper in the West; [Magnus Maximus](/wiki/Magnus_Maximus "Magnus Maximus") declared himself Emperor in 383, stripped troops from the outlying regions of [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") (probably replacing some with [federate chieftains](/wiki/Cunedda "Cunedda") and their war-bands) and invaded Gaul. His troops killed Gratian and he was accepted as Augustus in the Gallic provinces, where he was responsible for the first official executions of [Christian heretics](/wiki/Priscillian "Priscillian").[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964164-141) To compensate the Western court for the loss of Gaul, Hispania, and Britannia, Theodosius ceded the [diocese of Dacia](/wiki/Diocese_of_Dacia "Diocese of Dacia") and the [diocese of Macedonia](/wiki/Diocese_of_Macedonia "Diocese of Macedonia") to their control. In 387 Maximus invaded Italy, forcing [Valentinian II](/wiki/Valentinian_II "Valentinian II") to flee to the East, where he accepted Nicene Christianity. Maximus boasted to [Ambrose](/wiki/Ambrose "Ambrose") of the numbers of barbarians in his forces, and hordes of Goths, Huns, and Alans followed Theodosius.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964159-142) Maximus negotiated with Theodosius for acceptance as *Augustus* of the West, but Theodosius refused, gathered his armies, and counterattacked, [winning the civil war](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Save "Battle of the Save") in 388. There were heavy troop losses on both sides of the conflict. Later Welsh legend has Maximus's defeated troops resettled in [Armorica](/wiki/Armorica "Armorica"), instead of returning to Britannia, and by 400, Armorica was controlled by [Bagaudae](/wiki/Bagaudae "Bagaudae") rather than by imperial authority.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988178-143)

Theodosius restored Valentinian II, still a very young man, as *Augustus* in the West. He also appointed [Arbogast](/wiki/Arbogast_(magister_militum) "Arbogast (magister militum)"), a pagan general of [Frankish](/wiki/Franks "Franks") origin, as Valentinian's commander-in-chief and guardian. Valentinian quarreled in public with Arbogast, failed to assert any authority, and died, either by suicide or by murder, at the age of 21. Arbogast and Theodosius failed to come to terms and Arbogast nominated an imperial official, [Eugenius](/wiki/Eugenius "Eugenius") (r. 392–394), as emperor in the West. Eugenius made some modest attempts to win pagan support,[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964162-123) and with Arbogast led a large army to fight another destructive civil war. They were defeated and killed at the [Battle of the Frigidus](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Frigidus "Battle of the Frigidus"), which was attended by further heavy losses; especially among the Gothic federates of Theodosius. The north-eastern approaches to Italy were never effectively garrisoned again.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994159-144)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Theodosius_I%27s_empire.png/250px-Theodosius_I%27s_empire.png)](/wiki/File:Theodosius_I%27s_empire.png)

The [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire") and [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") at the death of Theodosius I in 395

Theodosius died a few months later in early 395, leaving his young sons [Honorius](/wiki/Honorius "Honorius") (r. 393–423) and [Arcadius](/wiki/Arcadius "Arcadius") (r. 383–408) as emperors. In the immediate aftermath of Theodosius's death, the *[magister militum](/wiki/Magister_militum "Magister militum")* [Stilicho](/wiki/Stilicho "Stilicho"), married to Theodosius's niece, asserted himself in the West as the guardian of Honorius and commander of the remains of the defeated Western army. He also claimed control over Arcadius in Constantinople, but [Rufinus](/wiki/Rufinus_(consul) "Rufinus (consul)"), *[magister officiorum](/wiki/Magister_officiorum "Magister officiorum")* on the spot, had already established his own power there. Henceforward the Empire was not under the control of one man, until much of the West had been permanently lost.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964157–58,_169-145) Neither Honorius nor Arcadius ever displayed any ability either as rulers or as generals, and both lived as the [puppet rulers](/wiki/Puppet_ruler "Puppet ruler") of their courts.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964173-146) Stilicho tried to reunite the Eastern and Western courts under his personal control, but in doing so achieved only the continued hostility of all of Arcadius's successive supreme ministers.

## Under Honorius (395-423)

Without an authoritative ruler, the Balkan provinces fell rapidly into disorder (despite [Honorius](/wiki/Honorius "Honorius")'s best efforts). [Alaric](/wiki/Alaric_I "Alaric I") was disappointed in his hopes for promotion to *magister militum* after the [battle of the Frigidus](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Frigidus "Battle of the Frigidus"). He led armed Gothic tribesmen in a [revolt](/wiki/Revolt_of_Alaric_I "Revolt of Alaric I") and established himself as an independent power, burning the countryside as far as the [walls of Constantinople](/wiki/Walls_of_Constantinople "Walls of Constantinople").[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994153-147) Alaric's ambitions for long-term Roman office were never quite acceptable to the Roman imperial courts, and his men could never settle long enough to farm in any one area. They showed no inclination to leave the Empire and face the Huns from whom they had fled in 376. Meanwhile, the Huns were still stirring up further migrations, with migrating tribes often attacking the Roman Empire in turn. Alaric's group was never destroyed nor expelled from the Empire, nor acculturated under effective Roman domination.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006213–214,_217–218,_242,_255-148)[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964187-149)[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen198858–121chapter_2_Power_Effective-150)

### Stilicho's attempts to unify the Empire, revolts, and invasions (395–406)

[![The Monza diptych, Stilicho with his family](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Stilicho.jpg/250px-Stilicho.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stilicho.jpg)

An ivory [diptych](/wiki/Diptych "Diptych"), thought to depict [Stilicho](/wiki/Stilicho "Stilicho") (right) with his wife [Serena](/wiki/Serena_(wife_of_Stilicho) "Serena (wife of Stilicho)") and son [Eucherius](/wiki/Eucherius_(son_of_Stilicho) "Eucherius (son of Stilicho)"), ca. 395 ([Monza Cathedral](/wiki/Monza "Monza"))

Alaric took his Gothic army on what Stilicho's propagandist [Claudian](/wiki/Claudian "Claudian") described as a "pillaging campaign" that began first in the East.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames201454-151) Alaric's forces made their way along the coast to [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), where he sought to force a new peace upon the Romans.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJames201454-151) His march in 396 passed through [Thermopylae](/wiki/Thermopylae "Thermopylae"). [Stilicho](/wiki/Stilicho "Stilicho") sailed from Italy to [Roman Greece](/wiki/Greece_in_the_Roman_era "Greece in the Roman era") with his remaining mobile forces, posing a clear threat to [Rufinus](/wiki/Rufinus_(consul) "Rufinus (consul)")'s control of the Eastern empire. The bulk of Rufinus's forces were occupied with [Hunnic](/wiki/Huns "Huns") incursions in [Asia Minor](/wiki/Asia_Minor "Asia Minor") and [Syria](/wiki/Roman_Syria "Roman Syria"), leaving [Thracia](/wiki/Thracia "Thracia") undefended. Claudian reports that only Stilicho's attack stemmed the plundering, as he pushed Alaric's forces north into [Epirus](/wiki/Epirus "Epirus").[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKulikowski2019126-152) [Burns'](/wiki/Thomas_S._Burns "Thomas S. Burns") interpretation is that Alaric and his men had been recruited by Rufinus's Eastern regime, and sent to [Thessaly](/wiki/Thessaly "Thessaly") to stave off Stilicho's threat.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994159-144) No battle took place. [Zosimus](/wiki/Zosimus_(historian) "Zosimus (historian)") adds that Stilicho's troops destroyed and pillaged too, and let Alaric's men escape with their plunder.[[b]](#cite_note-Zosimus-153)

Many of [Stilicho](/wiki/Stilicho "Stilicho")'s Eastern forces wanted to go home and he had to let them go (though Claudian claims that he did so willingly).[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994154-154) Some went to Constantinople under the command of one [Gainas](/wiki/Gainas "Gainas"), a Goth with a large Gothic following. On arrival, Gainas murdered Rufinus, and was appointed *magister militum* for [Thrace](/wiki/Thrace "Thrace") by [Eutropius](/wiki/Eutropius_(consul_399) "Eutropius (consul 399)"), the new supreme minister and the only eunuch consul of Rome. Eutropius reportedly controlled Arcadius "as if he were a sheep".[[b]](#cite_note-Zosimus-153) Stilicho obtained a few more troops from the German frontier and continued to campaign ineffectively against the Eastern empire; again he was successfully opposed by Alaric and his men. During the next year, 397, Eutropius personally led his troops to victory over some [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns") who were marauding in Asia Minor. With his position thus strengthened, he declared Stilicho a public enemy, and he established Alaric as *magister militum per [Illyricum](/wiki/Illyricum_(Roman_province) "Illyricum (Roman province)")*. A poem by [Synesius](/wiki/Synesius "Synesius") advises the emperor to display manliness and remove a "skin-clad savage" (probably Alaric) from the councils of power and his barbarians from the Roman army. We do not know if Arcadius ever became aware of the existence of this advice, but it had no recorded effect.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994162–163-155) Synesius, from a province suffering the widespread ravages of a few poor but greedy barbarians, also complained of "the peacetime war, one almost worse than the barbarian war and arising from military indiscipline and the officer's greed."[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen1988189-156)

[![406 representation of Honorius attended by a winged Victory on a globe and bearing a labarum with the words In nomine XRI vincas semper, lit. 'In the name of Christ thou wilt always conquer'](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Consular_diptych_Probus_406.jpg/250px-Consular_diptych_Probus_406.jpg)](/wiki/File:Consular_diptych_Probus_406.jpg)

A contemporary depiction of [Honorius](/wiki/Honorius "Honorius") on a [consular diptych](/wiki/Consular_diptych "Consular diptych") issued by [Anicius Petronius Probus](/wiki/Anicius_Petronius_Probus "Anicius Petronius Probus") to celebrate Probus's consulship in 406, now in the [Aosta](/wiki/Aosta "Aosta") museum

The *magister militum* in the [Diocese of Africa](/wiki/Diocese_of_Africa "Diocese of Africa") [declared for the East](/wiki/Gildonic_revolt "Gildonic revolt") and stopped the supply of grain to Rome.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994159-144) Italy had not fed itself for centuries and could not do so now. In 398, Stilicho sent his last reserves, a few thousand men, to re-take the Diocese of Africa. He strengthened his position further when he married [his daughter Maria](/wiki/Maria_(empress) "Maria (empress)") to Honorius. Throughout this period Stilicho, and all other generals, were desperately short of recruits and supplies for them.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994183-157) In 400, Stilicho was charged to press into service any "[laetus](/wiki/Laetus "Laetus"), Alamannus, Sarmatian, vagrant, son of a veteran" or any other person liable to serve.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994186-158) He had reached the bottom of his recruitment pool.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994187-159) Though personally not corrupt, he was very active in confiscating assets;[[b]](#cite_note-Zosimus-153) the financial and administrative machine was not producing enough support for the army.

In 399, [Tribigild's rebellion](/wiki/Gothic_Revolt_of_Tribigild "Gothic Revolt of Tribigild") in Asia Minor allowed Gainas to accumulate a significant army (mostly Goths), become supreme in the Eastern court, and execute Eutropius.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994169-160) He now felt that he could dispense with Alaric's services and he nominally transferred Alaric's province to the West. This administrative change removed Alaric's Roman rank and his entitlement to legal provisioning for his men, leaving his army—the only significant force in the ravaged Balkans—as a problem for Stilicho.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994175-161) In 400, the citizens of Constantinople revolted against Gainas and massacred as many of his people, soldiers and their families, as they could catch. Some Goths at least built rafts and tried to cross the strip of sea that separates Asia from Europe; the [Roman navy](/wiki/Roman_navy "Roman navy") slaughtered them.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard-Perkins200560-162) By the beginning of 401, Gainas' head rode a pike through Constantinople while [another Gothic general](/wiki/Fravitta "Fravitta") became consul.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994173-163) Meanwhile, groups of Huns started a series of attacks across the Danube, and the [Isaurians](/wiki/Isaurians "Isaurians") marauded far and wide in Anatolia.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964192-164)

In 401 Stilicho travelled over the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") to [Raetia](/wiki/Raetia "Raetia"), to scrape up further troops.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994191-165) He left the Rhine defended only by the "dread" of Roman retaliation, rather than by adequate forces able to take the field.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994191-165) Early in spring, Alaric, probably desperate,[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994190-166) invaded Italy, and he drove Honorius westward from [Mediolanum](/wiki/Mediolanum "Mediolanum"), besieging him in [Hasta Pompeia](/wiki/Hasta_Pompeia "Hasta Pompeia") in [Liguria](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria"). Stilicho returned as soon as the passes had cleared, meeting Alaric in two battles (near [Pollentia](/wiki/Battle_of_Pollentia "Battle of Pollentia") and [Verona](/wiki/Battle_of_Verona_(402) "Battle of Verona (402)")) without decisive results. The Goths, weakened, were allowed to retreat back to Illyricum where the Western court again gave Alaric office, though only as [comes](/wiki/Comes "Comes") and only over [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia_(Roman_province) "Dalmatia (Roman province)") and [Pannonia Secunda](/wiki/Pannonia_Secunda "Pannonia Secunda") rather than the whole of Illyricum.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994193-167) Stilicho probably supposed that this pact would allow him to put Italian government into order and recruit fresh troops.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994183-157) He may also have planned with Alaric's help to relaunch his attempts to gain control over the Eastern court.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994195-168)

[![Chi-rho pendant of Empress Maria, daughter of Stilicho and wife of Honorius.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/ChristianPendantMaria398-407.JPG/250px-ChristianPendantMaria398-407.JPG)](/wiki/File:ChristianPendantMaria398-407.JPG)

[Chi-rho](/wiki/Chi-rho "Chi-rho") pendant of Empress [Maria](/wiki/Maria_(empress) "Maria (empress)"), daughter of [Stilicho](/wiki/Stilicho "Stilicho"), and wife of Honorius, now in the [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre"), Paris. The pendant reads, around a central cross (clockwise):  
HONORI  
[MARIA](/wiki/Maria_(empress) "Maria (empress)")  
[SERHNA](/wiki/Serena_(wife_of_Stilicho) "Serena (wife of Stilicho)")  
VIVATIS  
[STELICHO](/wiki/Stilicho "Stilicho").  
The letters form a [Christogram](/wiki/Christogram "Christogram").

However, in 405, Stilicho was distracted by a fresh invasion of Northern Italy. Another group of Goths fleeing the Huns, led by one [Radagaisus](/wiki/Radagaisus "Radagaisus"), started the [War of Radagaisus](/wiki/War_of_Radagaisus "War of Radagaisus") and devastated the north of Italy for six months before Stilicho could muster enough forces to take the field against them. Stilicho recalled troops from [Britannia](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"), and the depth of the crisis was shown when he urged all Roman soldiers to allow their personal slaves to fight beside them.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994195-168) His forces, including Huns and Alans, may in the end have totalled rather less than 15,000 men.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994198-169) Radagaisus was defeated and executed, while 12,000 prisoners from the defeated horde were drafted into Stilicho's service.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994198-169) Stilicho continued negotiations with Alaric; [Flavius Aetius](/wiki/Flavius_Aetius "Flavius Aetius"), son of one of Stilicho's major supporters, was sent as a hostage to Alaric in 405.

In 406, Stilicho heard of [new invaders](/wiki/Crossing_of_the_Rhine "Crossing of the Rhine") and [rebels](/wiki/Constantine_III_(Western_Roman_Emperor) "Constantine III (Western Roman Emperor)") who had appeared in the northern provinces. He insisted on making peace with Alaric, probably on the basis that Alaric would prepare to move either against the Eastern court or against the rebels in Gaul. The Senate deeply resented peace with Alaric.

In 407, Alaric marched into [Noricum](/wiki/Noricum "Noricum") and demanded a large payment for his expensive efforts in Stilicho's interests. The senate, "inspired by the courage, rather than the wisdom, of their predecessors,"[[168]](#cite_note-170) preferred war.
