One senator famously declaimed *Non est ista pax, sed pactio servitutis* ("This is not peace, but a pact of servitude").[[169]](#cite_note-171) Stilicho paid Alaric four thousand pounds of gold nevertheless.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994215-172) Stilicho sent [Sarus](/wiki/Sarus_(Goth) "Sarus (Goth)"), a Gothic general, over the Alps to face the usurper [Constantine III](/wiki/Constantine_III_(Western_Roman_Emperor) "Constantine III (Western Roman Emperor)"). Sarus lost this campaign and barely escaped, having to leave his baggage to the bandits who now infested the Alpine passes.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994215-172)

The [empress Maria](/wiki/Maria_(empress) "Maria (empress)"), daughter of Stilicho, died in 407 or early 408 and her sister [Aemilia Materna Thermantia](/wiki/Thermantia "Thermantia") married Honorius. In the East, Arcadius died on 1 May 408 and was replaced by his son [Theodosius II](/wiki/Theodosius_II "Theodosius II"). Stilicho seems to have planned to march to Constantinople, and to install there a regime loyal to himself.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994216-173) He may also have intended to give Alaric a senior official position, and to send him against the rebels in Gaul. Before he could do so, while he was away at [Ticinum](/wiki/Ticinum "Ticinum") at the head of a small detachment, a bloody [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") against his supporters took place at Honorius's court. It was led by Stilicho's own creature, one [Olympius](/wiki/Olympius "Olympius").[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994218-174)

The ineffectiveness of Roman military responses during Stilicho's rule and afterwards has been described as "shocking".[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacgeorge2002171-175) There is little evidence of indigenous [field forces](/wiki/Field_Force "Field Force") or of adequate training, discipline, pay, or supply for the barbarians who formed most of the available troops. Local defence was occasionally effective, but was often associated with withdrawal from central control and taxes. In many areas, barbarians under Roman authority attacked culturally-Roman "[Bagaudae](/wiki/Bagaudae "Bagaudae")".[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006213–214,_217–218,_242,_255-148)[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964187-149)[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacMullen198858–121chapter_2:_Power_Effective-176) The fifth-century Western emperors, with brief exceptions, were individuals incapable of ruling effectively or even of controlling their own courts.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964173-146) Those exceptions were responsible for brief, but remarkable resurgences of Roman power.

### Stilicho's fall to the sack of Rome: end of effective regular field armies, starvation in Italy (408-410)

#### Alaric's reaction

Prior to his death, Stilicho had news of the coup at [Bononia](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), where he was probably waiting for Alaric.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994227-177) His army of barbarian troops, including a guard of Huns and many Goths under Sarus, discussed attacking the forces of the coup, but Stilicho prevented them when he heard that the Emperor had not been harmed. Sarus's Gothic troops then massacred the Hun contingent in their sleep, and Stilicho withdrew from the quarreling remains of his army to Ravenna. He ordered that his former soldiers should not be admitted into the cities in which their families were billeted. Stilicho was forced to flee to a church for sanctuary, promised his life, and killed.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994219-178)

Alaric was again declared an enemy of the Emperor. The conspiracy then massacred the families of the federate troops (as presumed supporters of Stilicho, although they had probably rebelled against him), and the troops defected *[en masse](/wiki/En_masse "En masse")* to Alaric.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994224–225-179) The conspirators seem to have let their main army disintegrate,[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994228-180) and had no policy except hunting down anyone they regarded as supporters of Stilicho.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994236-181) Italy was left without effective indigenous defence forces thereafter.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacgeorge2002171-175) [Heraclianus](/wiki/Heraclianus "Heraclianus"), a co-conspirator of Olympius, became governor of the Diocese of Africa. He consequently controlled the source of most of Italy's grain, and he supplied food only in the interests of Honorius's regime.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007216-182)

As a declared 'enemy of the Emperor', Alaric was denied the legitimacy that he needed to collect taxes and hold cities without large garrisons, which he could not afford to detach. He again offered to move his men, this time to [Pannonia](/wiki/Pannonia "Pannonia"), in exchange for a modest sum of money and the modest title of [Comes](/wiki/Comes "Comes"). He was refused, as Olympius's clique still regarded him as a supporter of Stilicho.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994226–227-183) He moved into Italy, probably using the route and supplies arranged for him by Stilicho,[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994227-177) bypassing the imperial court in [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna") which was protected by widespread marshland and had a port, and he menaced the city of Rome itself. In 407, there was no equivalent of the determined response to the catastrophic [Battle of Cannae](/wiki/Battle_of_Cannae#Aftermath "Battle of Cannae") in 216 BCE, when the entire Roman population, even slaves, had been mobilized to resist the enemy.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConnolly1998189-184)

Alaric's military operations centred on [the port of Rome](/wiki/Portus "Portus"), through which Rome's grain supply had to pass. Alaric's [first siege of Rome](/wiki/Sack_of_Rome_(410)#First_siege_of_Rome "Sack of Rome (410)") in 408 caused dreadful famine within the walls. It was ended by a payment that, though large, was less than one of the richest senators could have produced.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994233–234-185) The super-rich aristocrats made little contribution; pagan temples were stripped of ornaments to make up the total. With promises of freedom, Alaric also recruited many of the slaves in Rome.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994234-186)

Alaric withdrew to [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany") and recruited more slaves.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994234-186) [Athaulf](/wiki/Athaulf "Athaulf"), a Goth nominally in Roman service and brother-in-law to Alaric, marched through Italy to join Alaric. A small force of Hunnic mercenaries led by Olympius killed some of Athaulf's men on this journey. Sarus was an enemy of Athaulf, and on Athaulf's arrival went back into imperial service.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006227-187)

#### Alaric besieges Rome

In 409 Olympius fell to further intrigue, having his ears cut off before he was beaten to death. Alaric tried again to negotiate with Honorius, but his demands (now even more moderate, only frontier land and food,[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006226-188)) were inflated by the messenger and Honorius responded with insults, which were reported *verbatim* to Alaric.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994239-189) He broke off negotiations and the standoff continued. Honorius's court made overtures to the usurper [Constantine III](/wiki/Constantine_III_(Western_Roman_Emperor) "Constantine III (Western Roman Emperor)") in Gaul and arranged to bring Hunnic forces into Italy, Alaric ravaged Italy outside the fortified cities (which he could not garrison), and the Romans refused open battle (for which they had inadequate forces).[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994238–239-190) Late in the year, Alaric sent bishops to express his readiness to leave Italy if Honorius would only grant his people a supply of grain. Honorius, sensing weakness, flatly refused.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994240-191)

Alaric moved to Rome and captured [Galla Placidia](/wiki/Galla_Placidia "Galla Placidia"), sister of Honorius. The Senate in Rome, despite its loathing for Alaric, was now desperate enough to give him almost anything he wanted. They had no food to offer, but they tried to give him imperial legitimacy; with the Senate's acquiescence, he elevated [Priscus Attalus](/wiki/Priscus_Attalus "Priscus Attalus") as his puppet emperor, and he marched on Ravenna. Honorius was planning to flee to Constantinople when a reinforcing army of 4,000 soldiers from the East disembarked in Ravenna.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994242-192) These garrisoned the walls and Honorius held on. He had Constantine's principal court supporter executed and Constantine abandoned plans to march to Honorius's defence.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994243-193) Attalus failed to establish his control over the Diocese of Africa, and no grain arrived in Rome where the famine became even more frightful.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994243–244-194) [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome") reports [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism") within the walls.[[193]](#cite_note-Jerome-195) Attalus brought Alaric no real advantage, failing also to come to any useful agreement with Honorius (to whom Attalus offered mutilation, humiliation, and exile). Indeed, Attalus's claim was a marker of threat to Honorius, and Alaric dethroned him after a few months.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006239-196)

In 410 Alaric [took Rome](/wiki/Sack_of_Rome_(410) "Sack of Rome (410)") by starvation, and sacked it for three days. He invited its remaining barbarian slaves to join him, which many did. There was relatively little destruction. In some Christian holy places, Alaric's men even refrained from wanton violence, and Jerome tells the story of a virgin who was escorted to [a church](/wiki/Basilica_of_Saint_Paul_Outside_the_Walls "Basilica of Saint Paul Outside the Walls") by the invaders, after they had given her mother a beating from which she later died. The city of Rome was the seat of the richest senatorial noble families and the centre of their cultural patronage. To pagans it was the sacred origin of the empire, and to Christians the seat of the heir of [Saint Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter"). At the time, this position was held by [Pope Innocent I](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_I "Pope Innocent I"), the most authoritative bishop of the West. Rome had not fallen to an enemy since the [Battle of the Allia](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Allia "Battle of the Allia"), over eight centuries before. [Refugees](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee") spread the news and their stories throughout the Empire, and the meaning of the fall was debated with religious fervour. Both Christians and pagans wrote embittered tracts, blaming paganism or Christianity respectively for the loss of Rome's supernatural protection and all attacking Stilicho's earthly failures.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994228–231-197)[[b]](#cite_note-Zosimus-153) Some Christian responses anticipated the imminence of the [Last Judgment](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment"). [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") in his book "[City of God](/wiki/City_of_God_(book) "City of God (book)")" ultimately rejected the pagan and Christian idea that religion should have worldly benefits. He instead developed the doctrine that the City of God in heaven, undamaged by mundane disasters, was the true objective of Christians.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006229–232-198) More practically, Honorius was briefly persuaded to set aside the laws forbidding pagans to be military officers, so that one Generidus could re-establish Roman control in [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia_(Roman_province) "Dalmatia (Roman province)"). Generidus did this with unusual effectiveness. His techniques were remarkable for this period, in that they included training his troops, disciplining them, and giving them appropriate supplies even if he had to use his own money.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994196,_237–238-199) The penal laws were reinstated no later than 25 August 410, meaning that the overall trend of repression of paganism continued.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994238-200)

[![A monument from the Forum Romanum describing Honorius as most excellent and invincible](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/DN_Honorio_Florentissimo.jpg/250px-DN_Honorio_Florentissimo.jpg)](/wiki/File:DN_Honorio_Florentissimo.jpg)

Inscription honouring Honorius, as *florentissimo invictissimoque*, the most excellent and invincible, 417–418, [Forum Romanum](/wiki/Forum_Romanum "Forum Romanum")

[Procopius](/wiki/Procopius "Procopius") mentions a story in which Honorius, on hearing the news that Rome had "perished", was shocked. The emperor thought that the news was in reference to his favorite [chicken](/wiki/Chicken "Chicken"), which he had named "Roma". On hearing that [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") itself had fallen, he breathed a sigh of relief:

> At that time they say that the Emperor Honorius in Ravenna received the message from one of the eunuchs, evidently a keeper of the poultry, that Roma had perished. And he cried out and said, "And yet it has just eaten from my hands!" For he had a very large cockerel, Roma by name; and the eunuch comprehending his words said that it was the city of Roma which had perished at the hands of Alaric, and the emperor with a sigh of relief answered quickly: "But I thought that my fowl Roma had perished." So great, they say, was the folly with which this emperor was possessed.

— **[Procopius](/wiki/Procopius "Procopius"), The Vandalic War (*De Bellis* III.2.25–26)**

#### The Goths leave Italy

Alaric then moved south, intending to sail to Africa. His ships were wrecked in a storm, and he shortly died of fever. His successor [Athaulf](/wiki/Athaulf "Athaulf"), still regarded as an usurper and given only occasional and short-term grants of supplies, moved north into the turmoil of Gaul. In this region, there was some prospect of food. His supergroup of barbarians are called the [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") in modern works: they may now have been developing their own sense of identity.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994245-201)

### The Crossing of the Rhine: partial loss of Britannia, Hispania, and Gaul (405–418)

The [Crossing of the Rhine](/wiki/Crossing_of_the_Rhine "Crossing of the Rhine") in 405/6 brought unmanageable numbers of Germanic and [Alan](/wiki/Alans "Alans") barbarians (perhaps some 30,000 warriors, 100,000 people[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006198-202)) into Gaul. They may have been trying to get away from the Huns, who about this time advanced to occupy the [Great Hungarian Plain](/wiki/Great_Hungarian_Plain "Great Hungarian Plain").[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006202–205-203) For the next few years these barbarian tribes wandered in search of food and employment, while Roman forces fought each other in the name of Honorius and a number of competing claimants to the imperial throne.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964185–189-204)

The remaining troops in Britannia elevated a succession of imperial usurpers. The last, [Constantine III](/wiki/Constantine_III_(Western_Roman_Emperor) "Constantine III (Western Roman Emperor)"), raised an army from the remaining troops in Britannia, invaded Gaul and defeated forces loyal to Honorius led by [Sarus](/wiki/Sarus_(Goth) "Sarus (Goth)"). Constantine's power reached its peak in 409 when he controlled Gaul and beyond, he was joint consul with Honorius[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994128-205) and his magister militum [Gerontius](/wiki/Gerontius_(magister_militum) "Gerontius (magister militum)") defeated the last Roman force to try to hold the borders of Hispania. It was led by relatives of Honorius; Constantine executed them. Gerontius went to [Hispania](/wiki/Hispania "Hispania"), where he may have settled the [Sueves](/wiki/Sueves "Sueves") and the Asding [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals"). Gerontius then fell out with his master and elevated one [Maximus](/wiki/Maximus_of_Hispania "Maximus of Hispania") as his own puppet emperor. He defeated Constantine and was besieging him in [Arelate](/wiki/Arelate "Arelate") when Honorius's general [Constantius](/wiki/Constantius_III "Constantius III") arrived from Italy with an army (possibly, composed mainly of Hun mercenaries).[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006244-206) Gerontius's troops deserted him, and he committed suicide. Constantius continued the siege, defeating a relieving army. Constantine surrendered in 411 with a promise that his life would be spared, and was then executed.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006205–212-207)

In 410, the Roman civitates of Britannia rebelled against Constantine and evicted his officials. They asked for help from Honorius, who replied that they should look to their own defence. While [the British](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons") may have [regarded themselves as Roman](/wiki/Romano-British_culture "Romano-British culture") for several generations, and British armies may at times have fought in Gaul, no central Roman government is known to have appointed officials in Britannia thereafter.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006251-208) The supply of coinage to the [Diocese of Britannia](/wiki/Diocese_of_Britannia "Diocese of Britannia") ceases with Honorius.[[207]](#cite_note-209)

In 411, [Jovinus](/wiki/Jovinus "Jovinus") rebelled and took over Constantine's remaining troops on the Rhine. He relied on the support of [Burgundians](/wiki/Burgundians "Burgundians") and Alans, to whom he offered supplies and land. In 413, Jovinus also recruited Sarus. Athaulf destroyed their regime in the name of Honorius, afterwards both Jovinus and Sarus were executed. The Burgundians were settled on the left bank of the Rhine. Athaulf then operated in the south of Gaul, sometimes with short-term supplies from the Romans.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994257-210) All usurpers had been defeated, but large barbarian groups remained un-subdued in both Gaul and Hispania.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006251-208) The imperial government was quick to restore the Rhine frontier. The invading tribes of 407 moved into Hispania at the end of 409; the Visigoths left Italy at the beginning of 412 and settled themselves around [Narbo](/wiki/Narbo "Narbo").

[Heraclianus](/wiki/Heraclianus "Heraclianus") was still in command in the diocese of Africa. He was the last member of the [clique](/wiki/Clique "Clique") which had overthrown Stilicho to retain power. In 413 he [revolted](/wiki/War_of_Heraclianus "War of Heraclianus") and led an invasion of Italy, and lost to a subordinate of Constantius. He then fled back to Africa, where he was murdered by Constantius's agents.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994257-210)

In January 414 Roman naval forces blockaded Athaulf in Narbo, where he married Galla Placidia. The choir at the wedding included Attalus, a puppet emperor without revenues or soldiers.[[209]](#cite_note-penelope.uchicago.edu-211) Athaulf famously declared that he had abandoned his intention to set up a Gothic empire, because of the irredeemable barbarity of his followers, and instead he sought to restore the Roman Empire.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994258–259-212)[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006239-196) He handed Attalus over to Honorius's regime for mutilation, humiliation, and exile. He also abandoned Attalus's supporters.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994259-213) One of them, [Paulinus Pellaeus](/wiki/Paulinus_Pellaeus "Paulinus Pellaeus"), recorded that the Goths considered themselves merciful because they allowed him and his household to leave destitute, but alive, without being raped.[[209]](#cite_note-penelope.uchicago.edu-211) Athaulf moved out of Gaul, to [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") where his infant son by Galla Placidia was buried, and where he was assassinated by one of his household retainers, possibly a former follower of Sarus.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994259–260-214)[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006240–241-215) His ultimate successor [Wallia](/wiki/Wallia "Wallia") had no agreement with the Romans; his people had to plunder in Hispania for food.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurns1994260-216)

#### Settlement of 418: barbarians within the empire

[![Areas allotted to or claimed by barbarian groups in 416–418](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Hispania_418_AD.PNG/330px-Hispania_418_AD.PNG)](/wiki/File:Hispania_418_AD.PNG)

Areas allotted to or claimed by barbarian groups in 416–418

In 416 Wallia reached agreement with Constantius; he sent Galla Placidia back to Honorius and received provisions, six hundred thousand *[modii](/wiki/Ancient_Roman_units_of_measurement#Dry_measure "Ancient Roman units of measurement")* of wheat.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006241-217) From 416 to 418,
[Wallia's Goths campaigned in Hispania](/wiki/Gothic_War_in_Spain_(416%E2%80%93418) "Gothic War in Spain (416–418)") on Constantius's behalf, exterminating the Siling [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals") in [Baetica](/wiki/Baetica "Baetica") and reducing the Alans to the point where the survivors sought the protection of the king of the Asding Vandals. (After retrenchment they formed another barbarian supergroup, but for the moment they were reduced in numbers and effectively cowed.) In 418, by agreement with Constantius, Wallia's Goths accepted land to farm in [Aquitania](/wiki/Aquitania "Aquitania").[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006242-218) Constantius also reinstituted an annual council of the [southern Gallic provinces](/wiki/Septem_Provinciae "Septem Provinciae"), to meet at [Arelate](/wiki/Arelate "Arelate"). Although Constantius rebuilt the western field army to some extent, he did so only by replacing half of its units (vanished in the wars since 395) by re-graded barbarians, and by garrison troops removed from the frontier.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006246–48-219) The *[Notitia Dignitatum](/wiki/Notitia_Dignitatum "Notitia Dignitatum")* gives a list of the units of the western field army c. 425. It does not give strengths for these units, but A. H. M. Jones used the Notitia to estimate the total strength of the field armies in the West at 113,000 : Gaul, "about" 35,000; Italy, "nearly" 30,000; Britain 3,000; in Spain, 10–11,000, in the diocese of Illyricum 13–14,000, and in the diocese of Africa 23,000.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964197-220)

Constantius had married the princess Galla Placidia (despite her protests) in 417. The couple soon had two children, [Honoria](/wiki/Honoria "Honoria") and [Valentinian III](/wiki/Valentinian_III "Valentinian III"). Constantius was elevated to the position of [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus_(honorific) "Augustus (honorific)") in 420. This earned him the hostility of the Eastern court, which had not agreed to his elevation.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthews1975378-221) Nevertheless, Constantius had achieved an unassailable position at the Western court, in the imperial family, and as the able commander-in-chief of a partially restored army.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006257-222)[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007234-223)

This settlement represented a real success for the Empire – a poem by [Rutilius Namatianus](/wiki/Rutilius_Namatianus "Rutilius Namatianus") celebrates his voyage back to Gaul in 417 and his confidence in a restoration of prosperity. But it marked huge losses of territory and of revenue; Rutilius travelled by ship past the ruined bridges and countryside of [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany"), and in the west the river [Loire](/wiki/Loire "Loire") had become the effective northern boundary of Roman Gaul.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007231–232-224) In the east of Gaul the Franks controlled large areas; the effective line of Roman control until 455 ran from north of [Cologne](/wiki/Colonia_Claudia_Ara_Agrippinensium "Colonia Claudia Ara Agrippinensium") (lost to the [Ripuarian Franks](/wiki/Ripuarian_Franks "Ripuarian Franks") in 459) to [Boulogne](/wiki/Itius_Portus "Itius Portus"). The Italian areas which had been compelled to support the Goths had most of their taxes remitted for several years.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006246-225)[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964204-226) Even in southern Gaul and Hispania large barbarian groups remained, with thousands of warriors, in their own non-Roman military and social systems. Some occasionally acknowledged a degree of Roman political control, but without the local application of Roman leadership and military power they and their individual subgroups pursued their own interests.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006274–278-227)

## Under Valentinian III (425-455)

### Fallout of Constantius' death: civil war, and partial loss of the Diocese of Africa (421–433)

Constantius died in 421, after only seven months as Augustus. He had been careful to make sure that there was no successor in waiting, and his own children were far too young to take his place.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006257-222) Honorius was unable to control his own court, and the death of Constantius initiated more than ten years of instability. Initially Galla Placidia sought Honorius's favour in the hope that her son might ultimately inherit. Other court interests managed to defeat her, and she fled with her children to the Eastern court in 422. Honorius himself died, shortly before his thirty-ninth birthday, in 423. After some months of intrigue, the [patrician](/wiki/Patrician_(ancient_Rome) "Patrician (ancient Rome)") [Castinus](/wiki/Castinus "Castinus") installed [Joannes](/wiki/Joannes "Joannes") as Western Emperor, but the Eastern Roman government proclaimed the child [Valentinian III](/wiki/Valentinian_III "Valentinian III") instead, his mother [Galla Placidia](/wiki/Galla_Placidia "Galla Placidia") acting as [regent](/wiki/Regent "Regent") during his minority. Once again a [civil war](/wiki/Roman_civil_war_of_425 "Roman civil war of 425") took place. Joannes had few troops of his own. He sent [Aetius](/wiki/Flavius_Aetius "Flavius Aetius") to raise help from the Huns. An Eastern army landed in Italy, captured Joannes, cut his hand off, abused him in public, and killed him with most of his senior officials. Aetius returned, three days after Joannes' death, at the head of a substantial Hunnic army which made him the most powerful general in Italy. After some fighting, Placidia and Aetius came to an agreement; the Huns were paid off and sent home, while Aetius received the position of *magister militum*.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006261-228)

Galla Placidia, as [Augusta](/wiki/List_of_Augustae "List of Augustae"), mother of the Emperor, and his guardian until 437, could maintain a dominant position in court, but [women in Ancient Rome](/wiki/Women_in_Ancient_Rome "Women in Ancient Rome") did not exercise military power, and she could not herself become a general. She tried for some years to avoid reliance on a single dominant military figure, maintaining a balance of power between her three senior officers, Aetius (*magister militum* in Gaul), [Count Boniface](/wiki/Count_Boniface "Count Boniface") (governor in the [Diocese of Africa](/wiki/Diocese_of_Africa "Diocese of Africa")), and [Flavius Felix](/wiki/Flavius_Felix "Flavius Felix") (*magister militum praesentalis* in Italy).[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006260-229) Meanwhile, the Empire deteriorated seriously. Apart from the losses in the Diocese of Africa, Hispania was slipping out of central control and into the hands of local rulers and [Suevic](/wiki/Suebi "Suebi") bandits. In Gaul the Rhine frontier had collapsed, the [Aquitanian Goths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") [revolted](/wiki/Gothic_revolt_of_Theodoric_I "Gothic revolt of Theodoric I") and launched further attacks on [Narbo](/wiki/Narbo "Narbo") and Arelate, and the Franks, increasingly powerful although disunited, were the major power in the north-east. Armorica was controlled by [Bagaudae](/wiki/Bagaudae "Bagaudae"), local leaders not under the authority of the Empire.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006283-230) Aetius at least campaigned vigorously and mostly victoriously, defeating aggressive Visigoths, Franks, fresh Germanic invaders, Bagaudae in Armorica, and a rebellion in Noricum.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006285-231) Not for the first time in Rome's history, a [triumvirate](/wiki/Triumvirate "Triumvirate") of mutually distrustful rulers proved unstable. In 427, [Felix tried to recall Boniface](/wiki/Roman_civil_war_of_427%E2%80%93429 "Roman civil war of 427–429") from Africa. Boniface refused, and overcame Felix's invading force. Boniface probably recruited some Vandal troops among others.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007240-232)

In 428 the Vandals and Alans were united under the able, ferocious, and long-lived king [Genseric](/wiki/Genseric "Genseric"); he moved his entire people to [Tarifa](/wiki/Tarifa "Tarifa") near Gibraltar, divided them into 80 groups nominally of 1,000 people (perhaps 20,000 warriors in total),[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006198-202) and crossed from Hispania to [Mauretania](/wiki/Mauretania "Mauretania") without opposition. It was the beginning of [Vandal conquest of Roman Africa](/wiki/Vandal_conquest_of_Roman_Africa "Vandal conquest of Roman Africa"). They spent a year moving slowly to [Numidia](/wiki/Numidia "Numidia"), defeating Boniface. He returned to Italy where Aetius had recently had Felix executed. Boniface was promoted to *magister militum* and earned the enmity of Aetius, who may have been absent in Gaul at the time. In 432 the two met at the [Battle of Ravenna](/wiki/Battle_of_Ravenna_(432) "Battle of Ravenna (432)"), which left Aetius's forces defeated and Boniface mortally wounded. Aetius temporarily retired to his estates, but after an attempt to murder him he raised another Hunnic army (probably by conceding parts of Pannonia to them) and in 433 he returned to Italy, overcoming all rivals. He never threatened to become an Augustus himself and thus maintained the support of the Eastern court, where Valentinian's cousin [Theodosius II](/wiki/Theodosius_II "Theodosius II") reigned until 450.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006290-233)

### Ascendancy of Aetius, and total loss of Carthage (433–454)

Aetius campaigned vigorously, somewhat stabilizing the situation in Gaul and in Hispania. He relied heavily on his forces of [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns"). With a ferocity celebrated centuries later in the *[Nibelungenlied](/wiki/Nibelungenlied "Nibelungenlied")*, the Huns slaughtered many [Burgundiones](/wiki/Burgundians "Burgundians") on the middle Rhine, re-establishing the survivors as Roman allies, the first [Kingdom of the Burgundians](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Burgundians "Kingdom of the Burgundians"). This may have returned some sort of Roman authority to [Trier](/wiki/Trier "Trier").[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007244-234) Eastern troops reinforced [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage"), temporarily halting the Vandals, who in 435 agreed to limit themselves to [Numidia](/wiki/Numidia "Numidia") and leave the most fertile parts of North Africa in peace. Aetius concentrated his limited military resources to defeat the Visigoths again, and his diplomacy restored a degree of order to Hispania.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006288-235) However, his general [Litorius](/wiki/Litorius "Litorius") was badly defeated by the Visigoths at [Toulouse](/wiki/Battle_of_Toulouse_(439) "Battle of Toulouse (439)"), and a new Suevic king, [Rechiar](/wiki/Rechiar "Rechiar"), began vigorous assaults on what remained of Roman Hispania. At one point Rechiar even allied with [Bagaudae](/wiki/Bagaudae "Bagaudae"). These were Romans not under imperial control; some of their reasons for rebellion may be indicated by the remarks of a Roman captive under [Attila](/wiki/Attila "Attila") who was happy in his lot, giving a lively account of "the vices of a declining empire, of which he had so long been the victim; the cruel absurdity of the Roman princes, unable to protect their subjects against the public enemy, unwilling to trust them with arms for their own defence; the intolerable weight of taxes, rendered still more oppressive by the intricate or arbitrary modes of collection; the obscurity of numerous and contradictory laws; the tedious and expensive forms of judicial proceedings; the partial administration of justice; and the universal corruption, which increased the influence of the rich, and aggravated the misfortunes of the poor."[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibbon1782Chapter_XXXIV:_Attila._Part_II.-236)

[Vegetius](/wiki/Vegetius "Vegetius")'s advice on re-forming an effective army may be dated to the early 430s,[[235]](#cite_note-237)[[236]](#cite_note-238)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[237]](#cite_note-239) (though a date in the 390s has also been suggested).[[238]](#cite_note-240) He identified many deficiencies in the military, especially mentioning that the soldiers were no longer properly equipped:

> From the foundation of the city till the reign of the Emperor Gratian, the foot wore cuirasses and helmets. But negligence and sloth having by degrees introduced a total relaxation of discipline, the soldiers began to think their armor too heavy, as they seldom put it on. They first requested leave from the Emperor to lay aside the cuirass and afterwards the helmet. In consequence of this, our troops in their engagements with the Goths were often overwhelmed with their showers of arrows. Nor was the necessity of obliging the infantry to resume their cuirasses and helmets discovered, notwithstanding such repeated defeats, which brought on the destruction of so many great cities. Troops, defenseless and exposed to all the weapons of the enemy, are more disposed to fly than fight. What can be expected from a foot-archer without cuirass or helmet, who cannot hold at once his bow and shield; or from the ensigns whose bodies are naked, and who cannot at the same time carry a shield and the colors? The foot soldier finds the weight of a cuirass and even of a helmet intolerable. This is because he is so seldom exercised and rarely puts them on.[[239]](#cite_note-241)

A religious polemic of about this time complains bitterly of the oppression and extortion[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964173-146) suffered by all but the richest Romans. Many wished to flee to the Bagaudae or even to foul-smelling barbarians. "Although these men differ in customs and language from those with whom they have taken refuge, and are unaccustomed too, if I may say so, to the nauseous odor of the bodies and clothing of the barbarians, yet they prefer the strange life they find there to the injustice rife among the Romans. So you find men passing over everywhere, now to the Goths, now to the Bagaudae, or whatever other barbarians have established their power anywhere ... We call those men rebels and utterly abandoned, whom we ourselves have forced into crime. For by what other causes were they made Bagaudae save by our unjust acts, the wicked decisions of the magistrates, the proscription and extortion of those who have turned the public exactions to the increase of their private fortunes and made the tax indictions their opportunity for plunder?"[[240]](#cite_note-242)

[Gildas](/wiki/Gildas "Gildas"), a 6th-century monk and author of *[De Excidio et Conquestu Britanniae](/wiki/De_Excidio_et_Conquestu_Britanniae "De Excidio et Conquestu Britanniae")*, wrote that "In the respite from devastation, the island [Britain] was so flooded with abundance of goods that no previous age had known the like of it. Alongside there grew luxury."[[241]](#cite_note-Gildas-243) Nevertheless, effective imperial protection from barbarian ravages was eagerly sought. About this time authorities in Britannia asked Aetius for help: "'To Aetius, thrice consul: the [groans of the British](/wiki/Groans_of_the_Britons "Groans of the Britons").' Further on came this complaint: 'The barbarians push us back to the sea, the sea pushes us back to the barbarians; between these two kinds of death, we are either drowned or slaughtered.' But they got no help in return."[[241]](#cite_note-Gildas-243)

The Visigoths passed another waymark on their journey to full independence; they made their own [foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_policy "Foreign policy"), sending princesses to make (rather unsuccessful) [marriage alliances](/wiki/Marriage_of_state "Marriage of state") with Rechiar of the Sueves and with [Huneric](/wiki/Huneric "Huneric"), son of the Vandal king [Genseric](/wiki/Genseric "Genseric").[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007247-244)

In 439, the Vandals moved eastward, temporarily abandoning Numidia. They [captured](/wiki/Battle_of_Carthage_(439) "Battle of Carthage (439)") [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage"), where they established the [Vandal Kingdom](/wiki/Vandal_Kingdom "Vandal Kingdom"), an independent state with a powerful navy. This brought immediate financial crisis to the Western Empire. The diocese of Africa was prosperous, normally required few troops to keep it secure, contributed large tax revenues, and exported wheat to feed Rome and many other areas.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006288–290-245) Roman troops assembled in [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"), but the planned counter-attack never happened. Huns attacked the Eastern empire,[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006291–292-246) and "the troops, which had been sent against Genseric, were hastily recalled from Sicily; the garrisons, on the side of Persia, were exhausted; and a military force was collected in Europe, formidable by their arms and numbers, if the generals had understood the science of command, and the soldiers the duty of obedience. The armies of the Eastern empire were vanquished in three successive engagements ... From the [Hellespont](/wiki/Hellespont "Hellespont") to [Thermopylae](/wiki/Thermopylae "Thermopylae"), and the suburbs of Constantinople, [Attila] ravaged, without resistance, and without mercy, the provinces of Thrace and Macedonia"[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibbon1782Chapter_XXXIV:_Attila._Part_I.-247) Attila's invasions of the East were stopped by the [Theodosian Walls](/wiki/Theodosian_Walls "Theodosian Walls"); at this heavily fortified Eastern end of the Mediterranean there were no significant barbarian invasions across the sea into the rich southerly areas of Anatolia, the Levant, and Egypt.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard-Perkins200554–62-248) Despite internal and external threats, and more religious discord than the West, these provinces remained prosperous contributors to tax revenue; despite the ravages of Attila's armies and the extortions of his peace treaties, tax revenue generally continued to be adequate for the essential state functions of the Eastern empire.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard-Perkins200558–62-249)[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964206–207-250)

Genseric settled his Vandals as landowners.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006293–294-251) In 442, he was able to negotiate very favourable peace terms with the Western court. He kept his latest gains and his eldest son [Huneric](/wiki/Huneric "Huneric") was honoured by betrothal to Valentinian III's daughter [Eudocia](/wiki/Eudocia_(daughter_of_Valentinian_III) "Eudocia (daughter of Valentinian III)"). She carried the legitimacy of the conjoined [Valentinianic](/wiki/Valentinianic_dynasty "Valentinianic dynasty") and [Theodosian](/wiki/Theodosian_dynasty "Theodosian dynasty") dynasties. Huneric's Gothic wife was suspected of trying to poison her father-in-law Genseric; he sent her home without her nose or ears, and his Gothic alliance came to an early end.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibbon1782Chapter_XXXV:_Invasion_By_Attila._Part_I._[httpwwwgutenbergorgfiles2571725717-hfiles733733-hgib3-35htm-252) The Romans regained [Numidia](/wiki/Numidia "Numidia"), and Rome again received a grain supply from Africa.

The losses of income from the Diocese of Africa were equivalent to the costs of nearly 40,000 [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") or over 20,000 [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry").[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006298-253) The imperial regime had to increase taxes. Despite admitting that the peasantry could pay no more, and that a sufficient army could not be raised, the imperial regime protected the interests of landowners displaced from Africa and allowed wealthy individuals to avoid taxes.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006295–297-254)[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964205–206-255)

### The Hunnic invasions (444–453)

In 444, the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns") were united under [Attila](/wiki/Attila "Attila"). His subjects included Huns, outnumbered several times over by other groups, predominantly [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples").[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006330-256) His power rested partly on his continued ability to reward his favoured followers with precious metals,[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006332-257) and he continued to attack the Eastern Empire until 450, by when he had extracted vast sums of money and many other concessions.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibbon1782Chapter_XXXIV:_Attila._Part_I._[httpwwwgutenbergorgfiles2571725717-hfiles733733-hgib3-34htm-258)

Attila may not have needed any excuse to turn West, but he received one in the form of a plea for help from [Honoria](/wiki/Justa_Grata_Honoria "Justa Grata Honoria"), the Emperor's sister, who was being forced into a marriage which she resented. Attila claimed Honoria as his wife, and half of the Western Empire's territory as his [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry"). Faced with refusal, he invaded Gaul in 451 with a huge army. In the bloody [battle of the Catalaunian Plains](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Catalaunian_Plains "Battle of the Catalaunian Plains"), the invasion was stopped by the combined forces of the barbarians within the Western empire. They were coordinated by Aetius, and supported by what troops he could muster. The next year, Attila invaded Italy and proceeded to march upon Rome. An outbreak of disease in his army, lack of supplies, reports that [Eastern Roman](/wiki/Eastern_Roman "Eastern Roman") troops were attacking his noncombatant population in [Pannonia](/wiki/Pannonia "Pannonia"), and, possibly, [Pope Leo I](/wiki/Pope_Leo_I "Pope Leo I")'s plea for peace induced him to halt this campaign. Attila unexpectedly died a year later (453) and his empire crumbled as his followers fought for power. The life of [Severinus of Noricum](/wiki/Severinus_of_Noricum "Severinus of Noricum") gives glimpses of the general insecurity, and ultimate retreat of the Romans on the Upper Danube in the aftermath of Attila's death. The Romans were without adequate forces; the barbarians inflicted haphazard extortion, murder, kidnap, and plunder on the Romans and on each other. "So long as the Roman dominion lasted, soldiers were maintained in many towns at the public expense to guard the boundary wall. When this custom ceased, the squadrons of soldiers and the boundary wall were blotted out together. The troop at [Batavis](/wiki/Passau "Passau"), however, held out. Some soldiers of this troop had gone to Italy to fetch the final pay to their comrades, and no one knew that the barbarians had slain them on the way."[[257]](#cite_note-259)

### Valentinian III's assassination , and the sack of Rome (454-455)

In 454, Aetius was personally stabbed to death by Valentinian. "[Valentinian] thought he had slain his master; he found that he had slain his protector: and he fell a helpless victim to the first conspiracy which was hatched against his throne."[[258]](#cite_note-260) Valentinian himself was murdered by the dead general's supporters a year later.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibbonChapter_XXXV:_Invasion_By_Attila._Part_III._[httpwwwgutenbergorgfiles2571725717-hfiles733733-hgib3-35htm-261) A rich senatorial aristocrat, [Petronius Maximus](/wiki/Petronius_Maximus "Petronius Maximus"), who had encouraged both murders, then seized the throne. He broke the engagement between the princess [Eudocia](/wiki/Eudocia_(daughter_of_Valentinian_III) "Eudocia (daughter of Valentinian III)") and [Huneric](/wiki/Huneric "Huneric"), heir to the Vandal throne. This amounted to a declaration of war with the Vandals. Petronius had time to send [Avitus](/wiki/Avitus "Avitus") to ask for the help of the Visigoths in Gaul[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006375–377-262) before a Vandal fleet arrived in Italy. Petronius was unable to muster any effective defence, tried to flee the city, and was torn to pieces by a mob who paraded the bits around on a pole. The Vandals [entered Rome](/wiki/Sack_of_Rome_(455) "Sack of Rome (455)"), and plundered it for two weeks. Despite the shortage of money for the defence of the state, considerable private wealth had accumulated since the previous sack in 410. The Vandals sailed away with large amounts of treasure and also with the princess Eudocia. She became the wife of one Vandal king and the mother of another, [Hilderic](/wiki/Hilderic "Hilderic").[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007256-263)

The Vandals conquered Sicily. Their fleet became a constant danger to Roman sea trade, and to the coasts and islands of the western Mediterranean.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibbon1782Chapter_XXXVI:_Total_Extinction_Of_The_Western_Empire.-264)

## Avitus, Majorian, and Ricimer (455-461)

### Rise and fall of Avitus: further losses in Gaul (455–456)

[Avitus](/wiki/Avitus "Avitus"), at the Visigothic court in [Burdigala](/wiki/Burdigala "Burdigala"), declared himself Emperor. He moved on Rome with Visigothic support. He gained acceptance by [Majorian](/wiki/Majorian "Majorian") and [Ricimer](/wiki/Ricimer "Ricimer"), commanders of the remaining army of Italy. This was the first time that a [barbarian kingdom](/wiki/Barbarian_kingdoms "Barbarian kingdoms") had played a key role in the imperial succession.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006379-265) Avitus's son-in-law [Sidonius Apollinaris](/wiki/Sidonius_Apollinaris "Sidonius Apollinaris") wrote [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda "Propaganda") to present the Visigothic king [Theoderic II](/wiki/Theoderic_II "Theoderic II") as a reasonable man with whom a Roman regime could do business.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006381-266) Theoderic's payoff included precious metal from stripping the remaining public ornaments of Italy,[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007260-267) and an unsupervised campaign in Hispania. There he not only defeated the Sueves, executing his brother-in-law Rechiar, but he also plundered Roman cities.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006381-266) The Burgundians expanded their kingdom in the [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône") valley, while the Vandals took the remains of the Diocese of Africa.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2006382–383-268) In 456, the Visigothic army was too heavily engaged in Hispania to be an effective threat to Italy. Ricimer had just destroyed a pirate fleet of sixty Vandal ships. Majorian and Ricimer marched against Avitus [in a civil war](/wiki/Roman_civil_war_of_456 "Roman civil war of 456"), and defeated him near [Placentia](/wiki/Piacenza "Piacenza"). He was forced to become Bishop of Placentia, and died (possibly murdered) a few weeks later.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007261-269)

### Resurgence under Majorian, and an attempt to recover Africa (457–461)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/MajorianEmpire.png/250px-MajorianEmpire.png)](/wiki/File:MajorianEmpire.png)

During his four-year reign Majorian reconquered most of Hispania and southern Gaul, meanwhile reducing the Visigoths, Burgundians and Suevi to federate status.

Majorian and Ricimer were now in control of Italy. Ricimer was the son of a Suevic king, and his mother was the daughter of a Gothic one, so he could not aspire to an imperial throne. After some months, allowing for negotiation with the new emperor of Constantinople and the defeat of 900 Alamannic invaders of Italy by one of his subordinates, Majorian was acclaimed as Augustus.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Majorian is described by Gibbon as "a great and heroic character".[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibbon1782Chapter_XXXVI:_Total_Extinction_Of_The_Western_Empire._Part_II.-270) He rebuilt the army and navy of Italy with vigour and set about recovering the remaining Gallic provinces, which had not recognized his elevation. He defeated the Visigoths at the [Battle of Arelate](/wiki/Battle_of_Arelate "Battle of Arelate"), reducing them to federate status and obliging them to give up their claims in Hispania; he moved on to subdue the Burgundians, the [Gallo-Romans](/wiki/Gallo-Roman_culture "Gallo-Roman culture") around [Lugdunum](/wiki/Lugdunum "Lugdunum") (who were granted tax concessions and whose senior officials were appointed from their own ranks), and the Suevi and Bagaudae in Hispania. [Marcellinus](/wiki/Marcellinus_(magister_militum) "Marcellinus (magister militum)"), magister militum in Dalmatia and the pagan general of a well-equipped army, acknowledged him as emperor and recovered Sicily from the Vandals.[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartindale1980708–710Chapter_Marcellinus_6-271) [Aegidius](/wiki/Aegidius "Aegidius") also acknowledged Majorian and took effective charge of northern Gaul. (Aegidius may also have used the title "King of the Franks").[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007266–267-272) Abuses in tax collection were reformed and the [city councils](/wiki/Curiales "Curiales") were strengthened. Both were actions necessary to rebuild the strength of the Empire, but disadvantageous to the richest aristocrats.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964241-273) Majorian prepared a fleet at [Carthago Nova](/wiki/Carthago_Nova "Carthago Nova") for the essential reconquest of the Diocese of Africa.

The fleet was burned by traitors, and Majorian made peace with the Vandals and returned to Italy. Here Ricimer met him, arrested him, and executed him five days later. [Marcellinus](/wiki/Marcellinus_(magister_militum) "Marcellinus (magister militum)") in Dalmatia and [Aegidius](/wiki/Aegidius "Aegidius") around [Soissons](/wiki/Soissons "Soissons") in northern Gaul rejected both Ricimer and his puppets and maintained some version of Roman rule in their areas.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007391-274) Ricimer later ceded [Narbo](/wiki/Narbo "Narbo") and its hinterland to the Visigoths in exchange for their help against Aegidius; this made it impossible for Roman armies to march from Italy to Hispania.

## Last years and end (461-476)

### Libius Severus (461-465)

After the [civil war of 461](/wiki/Roman_civil_war_of_461 "Roman civil war of 461") , Ricimer was the effective ruler of Italy (but little else) for several years. From 461 to 465 the pious Italian aristocrat [Libius Severus](/wiki/Libius_Severus "Libius Severus") reigned as a [puppet emperor](/wiki/Puppet_emperor "Puppet emperor"). There is no record of anything significant that he even tried to achieve, he was never acknowledged by the [Leo I](/wiki/Leo_I_(emperor) "Leo I (emperor)") from the East whose help Ricimer needed, and he died in 465, either naturally or executed by Ricimer.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Anthemius; an emperor and an army from the East (467–472)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Tremissis_Anthemius-RIC_2842.jpg/250px-Tremissis_Anthemius-RIC_2842.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tremissis_Anthemius-RIC_2842.jpg)

[Tremissis](/wiki/Tremissis "Tremissis") of Anthemius

After two years without a Western emperor, the Eastern court nominated [Anthemius](/wiki/Anthemius "Anthemius"), a successful general who had a strong claim to the Eastern throne. He arrived in Italy with an army, supported by Marcellinus and his fleet. Anthemius married his daughter [Alypia](/wiki/Alypia_(daughter_of_Anthemius) "Alypia (daughter of Anthemius)") to Ricimer, and he was proclaimed Augustus in 467. In 468, at vast expense, the Eastern empire assembled an enormous force to help the West retake the Diocese of Africa. Marcellinus rapidly drove the Vandals from [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia") and Sicily, and a land invasion evicted them from [Tripolitania](/wiki/Tripolitania "Tripolitania"). The commander in chief with the main force defeated a Vandal fleet near Sicily, and landed at [Cape Bon](/wiki/Cape_Bon "Cape Bon"). Here Genseric offered to surrender, if he could have a five-day truce to prepare the process. He used the respite to prepare a full-scale attack preceded by [fireships](/wiki/Fire_ship "Fire ship"), which destroyed most of the Roman fleet and killed many of its soldiers. The Vandals were confirmed in their possession of the Diocese of Africa. They soon retook Sardinia and Sicily. Marcellinus was murdered, possibly on orders from Ricimer.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007273-275) The [Praetorian prefect of Gaul](/wiki/Praetorian_prefecture_of_Gaul "Praetorian prefecture of Gaul"), [Arvandus](/wiki/Arvandus "Arvandus"), tried to persuade [Euric](/wiki/Euric "Euric") the new king of the Visigoths to rebel, on the grounds that Roman power in Gaul was finished anyway; the king refused.

Anthemius was still in command of an army in Italy. Additionally, in northern Gaul, a British army led by one [Riothamus](/wiki/Riothamus "Riothamus"), operated in imperial interests at the [battle of Déols](/wiki/Battle_of_D%C3%A9ols "Battle of Déols").[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007276–277-276) Anthemius sent his son [Anthemiolus](/wiki/Anthemiolus "Anthemiolus") over the Alps, with an army, to request the Visigoths to return [southern Gaul](/wiki/Gallia_Narbonensis "Gallia Narbonensis") to Roman control. This would have allowed the Empire land access to Hispania again. The Visigoths refused, and defeated the forces of both Riothamus and Anthemius at the [battle of Arles](/wiki/Battle_of_Arles_(471) "Battle of Arles (471)"); with the Burgundians, they took over almost all of the remaining imperial territory in southern Gaul.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ricimer then quarreled with Anthemius, and besieged him in Rome, which surrendered in July 472, after more months of starvation.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007277-277) Anthemius was captured and executed (on Ricimer's orders) by the Burgundian prince [Gundobad](/wiki/Gundobad "Gundobad"). In August, Ricimer died of a [pulmonary haemorrhage](/wiki/Pulmonary_haemorrhage "Pulmonary haemorrhage"). [Olybrius](/wiki/Olybrius "Olybrius"), his new emperor, named Gundobad as his patrician, then shortly died himself.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007278-278)

### Final emperors: puppets of warlords (472–476)

After the death of Olybrius there was a further interregnum until March 473, when [Gundobad](/wiki/Gundobad "Gundobad") proclaimed [Glycerius](/wiki/Glycerius "Glycerius") emperor. He may have made some attempt to intervene in Gaul; if so, it was unsuccessful.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007279-279)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Julius_Nepos_Tremissis.jpg/120px-Julius_Nepos_Tremissis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Julius_Nepos_Tremissis.jpg)

Tremissis of Julius Nepos

In 474 [Julius Nepos](/wiki/Julius_Nepos "Julius Nepos"), nephew and successor of the general Marcellinus, arrived in Rome with soldiers and authority from the eastern emperor [Leo I](/wiki/Leo_I_(emperor) "Leo I (emperor)"). By that time, Gundobad had left to contest the Burgundian throne in Gaul.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007279-279) Glycerius gave up without a fight, retiring to become bishop of [Salona](/wiki/Salona "Salona") in Dalmatia.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007279-279) Julius Nepos ruled Italy and Dalmatia from [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna"), and appointed [Orestes](/wiki/Orestes_(father_of_Romulus_Augustulus) "Orestes (father of Romulus Augustulus)"), a former secretary of Attila, as [magister militum](/wiki/Magister_militum "Magister militum").

In 475, Orestes promised land in Italy to various Germanic mercenaries, [Heruli](/wiki/Heruli "Heruli"), [Scirian](/wiki/Scirian "Scirian") and [Torcilingi](/wiki/Torcilingi "Torcilingi"), in exchange for their support. He drove Julius Nepos out of [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna") and proclaimed his own son Flavius Momyllus Romulus Augustus ([Romulus Augustulus](/wiki/Romulus_Augustulus "Romulus Augustulus")) as Emperor, on October 31. His surname 'Augustus' was given the diminutive form 'Augustulus' by rivals, because he was still a minor. Romulus was never recognized outside Italy as a legitimate ruler.[[278]](#cite_note-280)

In 476, Orestes refused to honour his promises of land to his mercenaries, who revolted under the leadership of [Odoacer](/wiki/Odoacer "Odoacer"). Orestes fled to the city of [Pavia](/wiki/Ticinum "Ticinum") on August 23, 476, where the city's bishop gave him sanctuary. Orestes was soon forced to flee Pavia, when Odoacer's army broke through the city walls and ravaged the city. Odoacer's army chased Orestes to [Piacenza](/wiki/Piacenza "Piacenza"), where they captured and executed him on August 28, 476.

On September 4, 476, Odoacer forced [Romulus Augustulus](/wiki/Romulus_Augustulus "Romulus Augustulus"), whom his father Orestes had proclaimed to be Rome's Emperor, to abdicate. The *[Anonymus Valesianus](/wiki/Anonymus_Valesianus "Anonymus Valesianus")* wrote that Odoacer, "taking pity on his youth" (he was then 16 years old), spared Romulus' life and granted him an annual pension of 6,000 [solidi](/wiki/Solidus_(coin) "Solidus (coin)") before sending him to live with relatives in [Campania](/wiki/Campania "Campania").[[279]](#cite_note-romanemperors-281)[[280]](#cite_note-gibbon406-282) Odoacer installed himself as ruler over Italy, and sent the Imperial insignia to Constantinople.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007280–281-283)

## Aftermath: rump states , barbarian kingdoms

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Europe_and_the_Near_East_at_476_AD.png/500px-Europe_and_the_Near_East_at_476_AD.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_and_the_Near_East_at_476_AD.png)

Europe and the Mediterranean in AD 476

By convention, the Western Roman Empire is deemed to have ended on 4 September 476, when [Odoacer](/wiki/Odoacer "Odoacer") [deposed Romulus Augustulus and proclaimed himself ruler of Italy](/wiki/Deposition_of_Romulus_Augustulus "Deposition of Romulus Augustulus"). This convention is subject to many qualifications. In Roman constitutional theory, the Empire was still simply united under one emperor, implying no abandonment of territorial claims. In areas where the convulsions of the dying Empire had made organized self-defence legitimate, [rump states](/wiki/Rump_state "Rump state") continued under some form of Roman rule after 476. [Julius Nepos](/wiki/Julius_Nepos "Julius Nepos") still claimed to be Emperor of the West, and controlled [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia_(Roman_province) "Dalmatia (Roman province)") until his murder in 480. [Syagrius](/wiki/Syagrius "Syagrius"), son of Aegidius, ruled the [Domain of Soissons](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Soissons "Kingdom of Soissons") until his murder in 486 , effectively ending the empire's remnants.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1964246-284) The indigenous inhabitants of [Mauretania](/wiki/Mauretania "Mauretania") developed [kingdoms of their own](/wiki/Mauro-Roman_Kingdom "Mauro-Roman Kingdom"), independent of the Vandals, and with strong Roman traits. They again sought imperial recognition with the reconquests of [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I"), and they later put up effective resistance to the [Muslim conquest of the Maghreb](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Maghreb "Muslim conquest of the Maghreb").[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007405–411-285) The [civitates](/wiki/Civitas "Civitas") of Britannia continued to look to their own defence as Honorius had authorized; they maintained literacy in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and other identifiably Roman traits for some time although they sank to a level of material development inferior even to their [pre-Roman Iron Age](/wiki/British_Iron_Age "British Iron Age") ancestors.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard-Perkins2005118-286)[[285]](#cite_note-287)[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007284–319-288)

[![The Ostrogothic Kingdom of Italy](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Ostrogothic_Kingdom.png/250px-Ostrogothic_Kingdom.png)](/wiki/File:Ostrogothic_Kingdom.png)

The [Ostrogothic Kingdom](/wiki/Ostrogothic_Kingdom "Ostrogothic Kingdom"), which rose from the ruins of the Western Roman Empire

Odoacer began to negotiate with the East Roman (Byzantine) emperor [Zeno](/wiki/Zeno_(emperor) "Zeno (emperor)"), who was busy dealing with unrest in the East. Zeno eventually granted Odoacer the status of [patrician](/wiki/Patrician_(ancient_Rome) "Patrician (ancient Rome)") and accepted him as his own [viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") of Italy. Zeno, however, insisted that Odoacer had to pay homage to Julius Nepos as the Emperor of the Western Empire. Odoacer never returned any territory or real power, but he did issue coins in the name of Julius Nepos throughout Italy. The murder of Julius Nepos in 480 (Glycerius may have been among the conspirators) prompted Odoacer to invade Dalmatia, annexing it to his [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(476%E2%80%93493) "Kingdom of Italy (476–493)"). In 488, the Eastern emperor authorized a troublesome Goth, [Theodoric](/wiki/Theodoric_the_Great "Theodoric the Great") (later known as "the Great") to take Italy. After several indecisive campaigns, in 493 Theodoric and Odoacer agreed to rule jointly. They celebrated their agreement with a banquet of reconciliation, at which Theodoric's men murdered Odoacer's, and Theodoric personally cut Odoacer in half.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007287-289)

The mostly powerless, but still influential Western [Roman Senate](/wiki/Roman_Senate "Roman Senate") continued to exist in the city of Rome under the rule of the [Ostrogothic kingdom](/wiki/Ostrogothic_kingdom "Ostrogothic kingdom") and, later, the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") for at least another century, before disappearing at an unknown date in the early [7th century](/wiki/7th_century "7th century").[[288]](#cite_note-290)

## Legacy

Main article: [Legacy of the Roman Empire](/wiki/Legacy_of_the_Roman_Empire "Legacy of the Roman Empire")

The Roman Empire was not only a political unity enforced by the use of military power; it was also the combined and elaborated civilization of the [Mediterranean Basin](/wiki/Mediterranean_Basin "Mediterranean Basin") and beyond. It included manufacture, trade, and architecture, widespread secular literacy, written law, and an international language of science and literature.[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalsall2007287-289) The Western barbarians lost much of these higher cultural practices, but their [redevelopment](/wiki/Medieval_renaissances "Medieval renaissances") in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") by polities aware of the Roman achievement formed the basis for the later development of Europe.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard-Perkins200587–122-291)

Observing the cultural and archaeological continuities through and beyond the period of lost political control, the process has been described as a [complex cultural transformation](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity"), rather than a fall.[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBowersock200187–122-292)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-140)**  \* Numerous literary sources, both Christian and pagan, falsely attributed to Theodosius multiple anti-pagan initiatives such as the withdrawal of state funding to pagan cults (this measure belongs to [Gratian](/wiki/Gratian "Gratian")) and the demolition of temples (for which there is no primary evidence).[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameron199346–47,_72-134)
   * Theodosius was also associated with the ending of the Vestal virgins, but twenty-first century scholarship asserts the Virgins continued until 415 and suffered no more under Theodosius than they had since Gratian restricted their finances.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETesta2007260-135)
   * Theodosius did turn pagan holidays into workdays, but the festivals associated with them continued.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2014229–232-136)
   * Theodosius was associated with ending the [ancient Olympic Games](/wiki/Ancient_Olympic_Games "Ancient Olympic Games"), which he also probably did not do.[[137]](#cite_note-Perrottet2004-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138) [Sofie Remijsen](/w/index.php?title=Sofie_Remijsen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sofie Remijsen (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sofie_Remijsen "nl:Sofie Remijsen")] says there are several reasons to conclude the Olympic games continued after Theodosius I, and that they came to an end under [Theodosius the second](/wiki/Theodosius_II "Theodosius II"), by accident, instead. Two extant scholia on Lucian connect the end of the games with a fire that burned down the temple of the [Olympian Zeus](/wiki/Temple_of_Olympian_Zeus,_Athens "Temple of Olympian Zeus, Athens") during Theodosius the second's reign.[[139]](#cite_note-Remijsen-139)
